EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported flash flooding in the Woodland Hills area. The heavy rain produced a significant mud and debris flow down the road. Five cars were stuck in the mud and one car was swept away. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front blew through the Mojave Desert, with strong west to southwest winds ahead of it and strong north winds behind it.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds blew down a light pole.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several areas of cold low pressure brought several rounds of snow and gusty winds to eastern California from early morning on the 23rd through the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of snowfall occurred from the 23rd through the evening of the 24th. The first round dropped a light snowfall, mainly above 5000 ft, while the second round dropped widespread snowfall totals of 10 to 20 inches from Susanville south, with the highest amounts near Portola and the Sierra Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several areas of cold low pressure brought several rounds of snow and gusty winds to eastern California from early morning on the 23rd through the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of snow occurred from the 23rd through early morning on the 25th. The first round affected areas mostly west of highway 395, with 18 to 34 inches of snowfall near the crest but only 4 to 6 inches near Lee Vining. The second round affected a larger area with heavy snowfall, including 8 to 14 inches around Lee Vining and 2 feet at the Mammoth Lakes Ranger Station. In addition to snowfall, gusty winds of 25 to 35 mph affected much of the area with up to 116 mph over the crest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level atmospheric disturbance that tracked across central California during the late afternoon and early evening of January 17th squeezed some drizzle out of the stratus, but the layer remained intact. The stratus persisted through the 18th before an upper-level trough brought some clearing to the west side of the San Joaquin Valley during the morning of January 19th. An offshore flow developed over California on the 20th, drying the airmass over the San Joaquin Valley and breaking up the stratus.||Although the fog and stratus kept central and southern San Joaquin Valley temperatures near or below normal, mountain and desert locations once again experienced above normal temperatures. Once the stratus broke up, central and south Valley temperatures also warmed to above normal for a few days before sufficient moisture had evaporated and resaturated the lowest levels of the atmosphere. While dense fog had been a nightly occurrence since January 21st, by the 24th, the fog was lingering well into the late morning with only partial afternoon clearing. Locations experienced near zero visibility at times during most of late January. This persistent dense fog continued for the next several days, affecting air travel into and out of the San Joaquin Valley and keeping high temperatures well below normal.
A winter storm brought up to a foot of new snow to the Sierra Nevada Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to a foot of new snow fell at the higher elevations of Mt Lassen and lesser amounts fell down to the 5000 foot elevation level.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought up to a foot of new snow to the Sierra Nevada Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to a foot of new snow fell at the higher elevations of the northern Sierra Nevada Mountains and lesser amounts fell down to the 5500 foot elevation level.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first in a series of winter snow storms struck northern California on this date.  The snow levels mostly stayed above populated areas and affected passes, but the Mount Shasta City area was an exception to this rule.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter N Tennant reported 6.0 inches of snow in 12 hours ending at 18/0640 PST.

A series of storm systems moving across the Pacific Northwest was to blame for the windy conditions experienced in Southern California in late April. A particularly strong jet moving across the Pacific brought periods of strong winds to the mountains and deserts. The winds were responsible for blowing sand on roadways and even overturning one small plane.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Palm Springs Airport measured a wind gust of 45 mph on the 21st. The gusty winds were responsible for blowing over a single-engine, Cessna 172 aircraft after it landed Thursday afternoon around 1230 PST. The pilot, the only passenger, was not injured. Also, Indian Canyon Drive was closed due to large amounts of blowing sand near the Whitewater Wash around 1500 PST on the 21st. It had recently been reopened after being closed just a couple of days prior for blowing sand as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first in a series of winter snow storms struck northern California on this date.  The snow levels mostly stayed above populated areas and affected passes, but the Mount Shasta City area was an exception to this rule.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No reports were received from CAZ080 since the snow fell above populated areas. However, based on rainfall reports in CAZ080 (1.68 inches in 24 hours ending at 18/1200 PST at Scott Mountain near Callahan RAWS and 1.52 inches in 24 hours ending at 18/1200 PST at Somes Bar RAWS) and numerous reports of heavy snow in adjacent zone CAZ080, it is assumed that warning criteria was also met in CAZ080.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second in a series of winter snow storms struck northern California on this date. The snow levels were a bit lower with this storm and more populated areas got significant snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cooperative observer at Mt. Shasta City reported 13.0 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 19/1900 PST, then another 13.6 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 20/1900 PST.  A spotter SSE Mount Shasta reported 8.0 inches of snow in 12 hours ending at 19/0800 PST, then another 8.0 inches in 12 hours ending at 19/2000 PST with some of it melting.  A spotter ESE Mount Shasta reported 18.0 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 19/1950 PST.  A spotter 6NW Mount Shasta reported 18.0 inches of snow in 12 hours ending at 20/0700 PST.  A spotter 4SW Weed reported 32.5 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 20/0700 PST, then another 5.5 inches in 9 hours ending at 20/1600 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second in a series of winter snow storms struck northern California on this date. The snow levels were a bit lower with this storm and more populated areas got significant snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Weed Fire Department reported 6.0 inches of snow on the ground with moderate snow still falling.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second in a series of winter snow storms struck northern California on this date. The snow levels were a bit lower with this storm and more populated areas got significant snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter 2SSW Etna reported 8.0 inches of snow in 12 hours ending at 20/0700 PST. A spotter 6S Etna reported 8.0 inches of snow overnight ending at 20/0900 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong westerly jet and associated shortwave trough moved quickly over the region accompanied by strong winds in the mountains and deserts, along with light rain over the coastal areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds began in the Coachella Valley between 0750 and 0850 PST, during which time a peak gust of 72 MPH was measured by the Whitewater RAWS. High winds ended between 1350 and 1450 PST. A downed power pole closed a portion of Indian Canyon Road and a tractor trailer overturned near the intersection of Interstate 10 and Highway 111, but no injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long lasting winter storm moved through northern California during this interval.  The snow level started off above pass level with heavy rain totals reported at lower elevations.  The snow level lowered to populated areas later in the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of the snow from this system fell above populated areas, so no reports were received from this zone.  However, based on numerous heavy snow reports at higher elevations from adjacent zone CAZ082 coupled with heavy rainfall reports at lower elevations in CAZ080, it is presumed that Heavy Snow warning criteria was met in CAZ080.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually cold air mass combined with Pacific moisture to bring widespread snow to unusually low elevations in Northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter 2SSW Etna reported 10.0 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 25/0800 PST. 4.0 inches fell between 24/1700 and 24/2100 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning on February 8th, a dry cold front passed over the area. Ahead of the front, gusty west winds developed over the Kern County deserts during the evening of the 7th. Behind the front, a northerly flow aloft again set up over the area. Cold air moving into the central California interior cooled temperatures by around 10 to 15 degrees in most locations; highs only reached the mid 50s to the lower 60s in the warmest locations, such as central and southern the San Joaquin Valley and the Kern County deserts. After the front passed, the west side of the San Joaquin Valley became quite windy during the daytime on the 8th, and some locations had wind|gusts around 45 to 50 mph. Low clouds banked against the foothills along the west side of the Southern Sierra Nevada and remained there much of the day.|The strong northwest to north winds along the west side of the San Joaquin Valley combined with a weaker southeast to south flow over the east side of the Valley to set up an eddy north of Fresno. Some of the clouds along the foothills briefly wrapped around this eddy, and were caught in the northwest flow over the west side of the Valley. As a result, these clouds moved southward from the foothills into parts of the San Joaquin Valley, including Fresno and Hanford.||From February 9th through the 11th, a ridge of high pressure was just off the coast, keeping a cool, dry northerly flow over the area. The reinforcing push of cold air caused morning low temperatures to cool to around freezing in quite a few locations across the San Joaquin Valley. The ridge moved onto the West Coast on the 12th, pushed by an upper-level trough moving into the Pacific Northwest. With the ridge over the central California interior, temperatures rose to around 10 to 15 degrees above normal; San Joaquin Valley temperatures reached as high as the|mid-70s. Bakersfield and Fresno reached their warmest temperatures for the month, with highs of 76 and 75 respectively. The ridge continued to slowly move to the east on the 13th; as a result, temperatures were slightly cooler than on the 12th.

Windy conditions occurred in the southern end of the San Joaquin Valley and Grapevine areas; for instance, a gust of 47 mph occurred at Bakersfield Meadows Field Airport on the 18th. A gust of 86 mph, with a sustained wind speed of 64 mph, occurred on Grapevine Peak during that evening. This wind blew down to|the base of the Grapevine, with wind gusts exceeding 70 mph at the California Highway Patrol weigh station at the south end of the San Joaquin Valley. These high winds even briefly hampered travel on Interstate 5 over the Grapevine and through Tejon Pass. The upper-level low associated with this storm remained off the central California Coast. CZ_NAME: EASTERN SIERRA SLOPES OF INYO COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful Pacific storm system produced high winds, including extreme downslope winds, and locally heavy high elevation snow over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twenty-two inches of snow fell in Aspendell (8500').

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak ridging returned to the area by the 20th, with dry, but relatively cool, conditions throughout the central California interior during the next few days. However, by February 24th, the next storm was approaching the California coast and weakening the ridge. Precipitation associated with this storm reached the region late on the 25th, with rain continuing into the 26th. Heavy rain caused some flooding in the south end iof the San Joaquin Valley, with road flooding reported as far north as the Lamont area. This was a cold storm system, bringing measurable snowfall to the foothills along the Southern Sierra Nevada and the mountains along the west side of the San Joaquin Valley. A half inch of snow fell at Granite Station in the foothills of the Kern County Sierra Nevada, at an elevation of 2600 feet. The higher elevations of the Southern Sierra Nevada received up to 3 feet with this storm. During the daytime of the 26th, trace amounts of snow were even observed in some areas of the San Joaquin Valley, including Delano, Taft, Coalinga, Arvin, and some locations near Bakersfield.|The end of the month remained cooler than average, with low temperatures falling to freezing or below due to the residual cold air behind the previous system. On the 27th, some locations, mainly north of Kern County, had minimum temperatures as low as the mid to upper 20s. Lows the next morning were a couple of degrees warmer, but were 32 degrees or colder in several locations.|In summary, February began dry and cool, followed by periods of mild temperatures throughout the central California interior. Until the middle of the month, there was no rain reported in Fresno and Bakersfield, and significant rain and snow did not occur throughout the region until the latter half of the month. Temperatures were cooler than average during this part of the month due to repeated influxes of cold air. Frosty to freezing conditions occurred at times throughout the central and southern San Joaquin Valley, especially during the last two days of the month.

CZ_NAME: SOUTHERN MONTEREY BAY AND BIG SUR COAST
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms from March provided the necessary moisture to cause a hillside to fail producing a debris flow weeks later.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A hillside south of Big Sur gave way weeks after the last heavy rainfall ended. A debris flow occurred 6.2 miles north of the San Luis Obispo County line along State Highway One. The road remained closed until June 9, 2011.

Precipitation associated with this storm reached the region late on the 25th, with rain continuing into the 26th. Heavy rain caused some flooding in the south end iof the San Joaquin Valley, with road flooding reported as far north as the Lamont area. This was a cold storm system, bringing measurable snowfall to the foothills along the Southern Sierra Nevada and the mountains along the west side of the San Joaquin Valley. A half inch of snow fell at Granite Station in the foothills of the Kern County Sierra Nevada, at an elevation of 2600 feet. The higher elevations of the Southern Sierra Nevada received up to 3 feet with this storm. During the daytime of the 26th, trace amounts of snow were even observed in some areas of the San Joaquin Valley, including Delano, Taft, Coalinga, Arvin, and some locations near Bakersfield.|The end of the month remained cooler than average, with low temperatures falling to freezing or below due to the residual cold air behind the previous system. On the 27th, some locations, mainly north of Kern County, had minimum temperatures as low as the mid to upper 20s. Lows the next morning were a couple of degrees warmer, but were 32 degrees or colder in several locations.|In summary, February began dry and cool, followed by periods of mild temperatures throughout the central California interior. Until the middle of the month, there was no rain reported in Fresno and Bakersfield, and significant rain and snow did not occur throughout the region until the latter half of the month. Temperatures were cooler than average during this part of the month due to repeated influxes of cold air. Frosty to freezing conditions occurred at times throughout the central and southern San Joaquin Valley, especially during the last two days of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The fourth in a series of winter snow storms struck northern California on this date. The snow levels were a bit higher with this storm and the only populated area that saw significant snow was the higher areas around Mount Shasta City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter 1W Mount Shasta reported 8.0 inches of snow in 11 hours ending at 26/0800 PST.  A spotter SSE Mount Shasta reported 7.5 inches of snow in 12 hours ending at 26/1000 PST.  The cooperative observer at Mount Shasta City reported 6.7 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 26/1900 PST, but most if not all of this likely fell in the first 12 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level atmospheric disturbance that tracked across central California during the late afternoon and early evening of January 17th squeezed some drizzle out of the stratus, but the layer remained intact. The stratus persisted through the 18th before an upper-level trough brought some clearing to the west side of the San Joaquin Valley during the morning of January 19th. An offshore flow developed over California on the 20th, drying the airmass over the San Joaquin Valley and breaking up the stratus.||Although the fog and stratus kept central and southern San Joaquin Valley temperatures near or below normal, mountain and desert locations once again experienced above normal temperatures. Once the stratus broke up, central and south Valley temperatures also warmed to above normal for a few days before sufficient moisture had evaporated and resaturated the lowest levels of the atmosphere. While dense fog had been a nightly occurrence since January 21st, by the 24th, the fog was lingering well into the late morning with only partial afternoon clearing. Locations experienced near zero visibility at times during most of late January. This persistent dense fog continued for the next several days, affecting air travel into and out of the San Joaquin Valley and keeping high temperatures well below normal.

Temperatures in the foothills fell below freezing during the morning of January 10th, and moisture from the fog froze to create icy road surfaces.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On March 10, a 9.1 earthquake off the west coast of Japan caused a tsunami that traveled across the Pacific Ocean in roughly ten hours.  The first waves arrived on the coast of northern California around 9:30 a.m. on March 11 and continued for over three days.  The largest amplitude wave arrived at approximately 10:30 a.m. on March 11 and measured approximately 8 feet in Crescent City, California. The maximum amplitude wave coincided with a relatively small 0.3 foot astronomical tide.  This resulted in a total water depth of approximately 8.3 feet above mean sea level which did not inundate any land surface.  The high velocity current associated with this maximum amplitude wave resulted in the majority of damage reported.  The highest overall water height occurred at 2:00 a.m. the following morning when a 3 foot tsunami wave coincided with a 6 foot astronomical tide.  The resultant overall water height of approximately 9 feet inundated a state highway but did not cause any damages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Surges in Crescent City harbor caused damage to docks and coastal infrastructure and sank numerous fishing boats.  The damage was primarily caused by the high velocity current.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful late season winter storm brought gusty winds, heavy snow and flash flooding to Southwestern California. Across the area, rainfall amounts were very significant, ranging from 3-7.5 inches across coastal and valley areas to 5-10 inches across the foothills and mountains. This heavy rainfall produced a couple reports of flash flooding. Cold air, associated with the storm, producing low snow levels. Snowfall totals in the mountains ranged between 1 and 4 feet. Gusty south to southwest were reported with some spots experiencing very strong wind gusts in excess of 70 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong southerly winds developed across the west coast of Santa Barbara county. A wind tower at Vandenberg Air Force Base reported a southerly wind gusts of 60 MPH at 385 feet elevation.

A strong cold upper level low pressure system moved southward from the Four Corners region into northern Mexico. Strong surface high pressure settled over the Great Basin and helped to bring strong offshore winds from the 2nd through the 4th of the month. The strongest winds occurred in and below the canyons and passes, causing downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated around 58 MPH were blamed for toppling a large Eucalyptus tree onto several parked vehicles in La Jolla. No one was injured. A semi was also blown over, blocking two lanes of eastbound Interstate 8 at Route 79. Again, no injuries. A downed power line near Ramona cause power outages to around 200 customers.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds began in Fremont Canyon between 1230 and 1330 PST, during which time a peak gust of 60 MPH was measured by the Fremont Canyon RAWS. High winds ended between 1530 and 1630 PST.|A tree, blown down by winds, blocked traffic for about 45 minutes on the westbound 22 connector to northbound I-5. No injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold storm system dropped south from the Gulf of Alaska, accompanied by a strong cold front packing gusty winds and moderate to heavy rain and snow. Snow fell as low as 1000 feet, with accumulations generally above 1500 feet. Winter Storm conditions developed in the mountains above 5000 feet where 15 to 30 inches of snow was reported by the end of the storm. Gusty winds also contributed to areas of blowing snow, further reducing visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain washed mud and debris onto sections of San Timoteo Canyon Road and Live Oak Canyon Roads. The roads were closed through the weekend to clean up. Cost of the cleanup is unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong and moist southwest to west flow from the Pacific Ocean brought heavy lower elevation rain and mountain snow on the 15th and 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A widespread snowfall of 10 to 20 inches was reported above 6500 feet with this system, with isolated amounts of 20 to 28 inches near the Sierra crest. Below 6500 feet, only 2 to 5 inches was reported as much of the precipitation was rain. The exception was on the west side of Lake Tahoe with the Tahoe City CO-OP reporting 9 inches.  Highways 89 and 50 were closed for a time on the 16th for avalanche control.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold low dropped slowly south from off the Washington coast on the 18th before moving into northern California on the 21st. This brought a couple rounds of heavy snow to the Sierra and northeast California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven inches of snow fell in Susanville while 13 inches fell in Portola on the 18th and early on the 19th.  An estimated 16 inches of snow fell in extreme southern Sierra County at the Independence Camp SNOTEL.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful late season winter storm brought gusty winds, heavy snow and flash flooding to Southwestern California. Across the area, rainfall amounts were very significant, ranging from 3-7.5 inches across coastal and valley areas to 5-10 inches across the foothills and mountains. This heavy rainfall produced a couple reports of flash flooding. Cold air, associated with the storm, producing low snow levels. Snowfall totals in the mountains ranged between 1 and 4 feet. Gusty south to southwest were reported with some spots experiencing very strong wind gusts in excess of 70 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong southerly winds developed across the south coast of Santa Barbara county. The RAWS sensor at Las Flores Canyon reported sustained winds of 41 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful late season winter storm brought gusty winds, heavy snow and flash flooding to Southwestern California. Across the area, rainfall amounts were very significant, ranging from 3-7.5 inches across coastal and valley areas to 5-10 inches across the foothills and mountains. This heavy rainfall produced a couple reports of flash flooding. Cold air, associated with the storm, producing low snow levels. Snowfall totals in the mountains ranged between 1 and 4 feet. Gusty south to southwest were reported with some spots experiencing very strong wind gusts in excess of 70 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong mountain wave developed across the Antelope Valley, producing very strong southwesterly winds. The sensor at Valyermo reported wind gusts between 59 and 77 MPH. The RAWS sensor at Lake Palmdale reported wind gusts between 58 and 63 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful late season winter storm brought gusty winds, heavy snow and flash flooding to Southwestern California. Across the area, rainfall amounts were very significant, ranging from 3-7.5 inches across coastal and valley areas to 5-10 inches across the foothills and mountains. This heavy rainfall produced a couple reports of flash flooding. Cold air, associated with the storm, producing low snow levels. Snowfall totals in the mountains ranged between 1 and 4 feet. Gusty south to southwest were reported with some spots experiencing very strong wind gusts in excess of 70 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong southerly winds developed across the Santa Monica Mountains. A weather spotter in Malibu reported a wind gust of 60 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The same cold trough that caused heavy snow less than 2 days before sent another wave of energy at the region for additional heavy snow. This system wrapped up an impressive snowfall period from the 15th through the 19th where an average of 5 to 8 feet, with local amounts to 10 feet of snow fell in the northern Sierra.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This period of heavy snow mainly affected areas south and west of Susanville, with an average of 6 to 11 inches in those areas with much less elsewhere (including only one inch in Susanville). The highest snowfall total was reported 3 miles southwest of Susanville with 11 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The same cold trough that caused heavy snow less than 2 days before sent another wave of energy at the region for additional heavy snow. This system wrapped up an impressive snowfall period from the 15th through the 19th where an average of 5 to 8 feet, with local amounts to 10 feet of snow fell in the northern Sierra.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals from the 17th through the morning of the 19th ranged from 35 to 45 inches near the Sierra crest down to 15 to 25 inches for the Truckee and South Lake Tahoe airports. Here are some specific totals: 31 inches at the Tahoe City CO-OP and 25 inches at the Fallen Leaf (near South Lake Tahoe) and Truckee SNOTELs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level atmospheric disturbance that tracked across central California during the late afternoon and early evening of January 17th squeezed some drizzle out of the stratus, but the layer remained intact. The stratus persisted through the 18th before an upper-level trough brought some clearing to the west side of the San Joaquin Valley during the morning of January 19th. An offshore flow developed over California on the 20th, drying the airmass over the San Joaquin Valley and breaking up the stratus.||Although the fog and stratus kept central and southern San Joaquin Valley temperatures near or below normal, mountain and desert locations once again experienced above normal temperatures. Once the stratus broke up, central and south Valley temperatures also warmed to above normal for a few days before sufficient moisture had evaporated and resaturated the lowest levels of the atmosphere. While dense fog had been a nightly occurrence since January 21st, by the 24th, the fog was lingering well into the late morning with only partial afternoon clearing. Locations experienced near zero visibility at times during most of late January. This persistent dense fog continued for the next several days, affecting air travel into and out of the San Joaquin Valley and keeping high temperatures well below normal.

CZ_NAME: EASTERN SIERRA SLOPES OF INYO COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold Pacific storm brought heavy snow to the higher elevations of the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Locally heavy rain also occurred in the valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter measured 12 inches of snow in Aspendell (8500').

CZ_NAME: EASTERN SIERRA SLOPES OF INYO COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front blew through the Mojave Desert, with strong west to southwest winds ahead of it and strong north winds behind it.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This gust occurred 4 miles SW of Independence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front blew through the Mojave Desert, with strong west to southwest winds ahead of it and strong north winds behind it.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several hours of high winds buffeted the western Mojave Desert.  The Bicycle Lake ASOS (KBYS) at Fort Irwin measured a gust to 69 knots at 4:55 AM, and the Barstow-Daggett Airport ASOS (KDAG) measured a gust to 56 knots at 1:29 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front blew through the Mojave Desert, with strong west to southwest winds ahead of it and strong north winds behind it.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This gust occurred at Twentynine Palms (KNXP).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving system dropped into northern California on the 4th. Most areas received a relatively light snowfall, however a narrow band of heavy snow developed near Portola and Graeagle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snowfall totals of 2 to 4 inches fell across eastern Plumas, Sierra, and extreme southern Lassen Counties on the 3rd and early on the 4th. However, a narrow band of heavy snow developed late in the evening of the 3rd and dropped 9 to 13 inches in the Portola and Graeagle areas.

CZ_NAME: LOS ANGELES COUNTY MOUNTAINS EXCLUDING THE SANTA MONICA RANGE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the last week of February, another powerful winter storm brought significant rain and snow to Southwestern California. The storm produced remarkable snowfall amounts in the mountains with some resorts receiving 48 inches of new snowfall. Snow levels with this storm were very low with some snowfall accumulations across the Antelope Valley and even across the foothill areas in the valleys (around 1700 feet). Rainfall totals with this storm generally ranged between 0.50 and 1.50 inches over coastal areas with between 1.50 and 3.50 inches of rainfall across the foothills and mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall and gusty winds produced dangerous winter storm conditions across the mountains of Los Angeles County. Above 6000 feet, snowfall totals ranged between 24 and 48 inches. Below 6000 feet, snowfall amounts ranged between 6 and 16 inches. Along with the heavy snowfall, southerly winds gusting between 40 and 50 MPH produced very hazardous winter storm conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the last week of February, another powerful winter storm brought significant rain and snow to Southwestern California. The storm produced remarkable snowfall amounts in the mountains with some resorts receiving 48 inches of new snowfall. Snow levels with this storm were very low with some snowfall accumulations across the Antelope Valley and even across the foothill areas in the valleys (around 1700 feet). Rainfall totals with this storm generally ranged between 0.50 and 1.50 inches over coastal areas with between 1.50 and 3.50 inches of rainfall across the foothills and mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall and gusty winds produced dangerous winter storm conditions across the mountains of Ventura County. Above 6000 feet, snowfall totals ranged between 12 and 24 inches. Below 6000 feet, snowfall amounts ranged between 3 and 8 inches. Along with the heavy snowfall, southerly winds gusting between 40 and 50 MPH produced very hazardous winter storm conditions.

CZ_NAME: EASTERN SIERRA SLOPES OF INYO COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two skiers were killed in an avalanche just east of the Sierra crest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two skiers died in an avalanche on the east-facing couloir of Split Mountain, near the Sierra crest southwest of Big Pine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong and gusty northerly winds developed across the Santa Ynez Range in southern Santa Barbara County. The RAWS sensor at Montecito reported sustained winds between 40 and 50 MPH with gusts between 58 and 67 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The RAWS sensor at Montecito reported northerly winds with sustained speeds between 40 and 50 MPH with gusts between 58 and 67 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong and gusty northwest to north winds developed across sections of Southwestern California. The strongest northwest winds developed across the Antelope Valley with gusts at high as 67 MPH reported. Strong north winds developed across the Santa Ynez Range (in Santa Barbara County) with sustained winds of 40 MPH reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS sensor at Lancaster reported west to northwest winds with sustained speeds between 40 and 49 MPH and gusts between 58 and 67 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong and gusty northwest to north winds developed across sections of Southwestern California. The strongest northwest winds developed across the Antelope Valley with gusts at high as 67 MPH reported. Strong north winds developed across the Santa Ynez Range (in Santa Barbara County) with sustained winds of 40 MPH reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The RAWS sensor at Las Flores Canyon reported north winds with a sustained speed of 40 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a prolonged warm and dry spell a strong low pressure trough made its way out of the Gulf of Alaska and into California. This trough began the series of cool weather systems that produced six consecutive days of precipitation in the San Francisco Bay Area and the central California coast. Damaging wind, locally flooding rain, intense hail and even moderate snowfall above 1500 feet were experienced during the first wave of this series.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty wind caused a tree to fall onto the westbound connector of State Route 4 to westbound Interstate 80 at approximately 5:05 a.m. PST. The roadway reopened at 7:00 a.m. PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a prolonged warm and dry spell a strong low pressure trough made its way out of the Gulf of Alaska and into California. This trough began the series of cool weather systems that produced six consecutive days of precipitation in the San Francisco Bay Area and the central California coast. Damaging wind, locally flooding rain, intense hail and even moderate snowfall above 1500 feet were experienced during the first wave of this series.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After four days of rain with over seven inches falling in the higher terrain, the Willow Brook Creek swelled beyond its banks causing minor flooding along its channel. The stream gages at Old Redwood Highway and Penngrove Park went over their flood stages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a prolonged warm and dry spell a strong low pressure trough made its way out of the Gulf of Alaska and into California. This trough began the series of cool weather systems that produced six consecutive days of precipitation in the San Francisco Bay Area and the central California coast. Damaging wind, locally flooding rain, intense hail and even moderate snowfall above 1500 feet were experienced during the first wave of this series.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain drenched San Francisco causing storm drains to back up which resulted in minor flooding throughout the city. The Public Utilities Commission reported 25 minor floods at various locations. Nineteen of those floods occurred at intersections and six occurred at homes or businesses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions prevailed over the area through March 15th, with gusts of 45-60 mph at times in the mountain and desert areas of Kern County. The weather pattern then changed sharply, transitioning to a 12-day period which saw periods of heavy precipitation and below normal temperatures.||From the evening of the 15th until the afternoon of the 16th, a storm system passed over the area, bringing precipitation mainly to the mountains and foothills, but light amounts did fall on the San Joaquin Valley floor. Most of the precipitation fell mainly as rain over the Southern Sierra Nevada in the northern part of WFO San Joaquin Valley-Hanford���s forecast/warning area. The highest amounts, which occurred in Yosemite National Park, ranged from about 1.5 to 3 inches; this was the most precipitation that had occurred in the region for the month thus far. However, this was just the harbinger of what was to come only 4 days later. ||While the rain was focused toward the north, winds were the main concern over the southern part of the region. The Kern County deserts saw gusts of 45-55 mph, and gusts as high as 66 mph were recorded in the Kern County mountains. The winds pushed clouds over the San Joaquin Valley into the Tehachapi Mountains, creating areas of dense fog. Visibilities as low as 100 feet were reported near Tehachapi.||The next storm arrived two days later. This was a two-part system, with the first part bringing between a tenth and a third of an inch of rain to the central and southern San Joaquin Valley. The first wave also brought a very cold airmass to the region. A foot of snow fell at Hetch Hetchy, and 10 inches of snow fell as far south as Lodgepole in Sequoia National Park. Snow even fell in the foothills, with 5 inches falling at Mid Pines at an elevation of 2400 feet. The main part of the storm arrived on the 20th, bringing record rain to both Bakersfield and Fresno, the latter city recording 1.82 inch of rain. In the Southern Sierra Nevada, there was no break in the snow, as upslope showers continued through the 19th until the second wave arrived.||Another foot of snow fell as far south as Fresno County on the 19th, and 4-5 inches in Tulare County. ||Strong winds developed ahead of the main storm, with gusts up to 84 mph in the hills above the Grapevine during the night of March 19th-20th, and gusts up to 60 mph at the lower elevations from Frazier Park to the base of the Grapevine.||The winds spread as far north as Bakersfield during the early morning of the 20th, and continued spreading north through the day. The heavy rains caused some road flooding on the San Joaquin Valley floor, as well as on Interstate 5 north of Fort Tejon before the mountain rain turned to snow. Up to 4 feet of snow fell at Frazier Park; the strong winds created drifts as high as 6 feet deep. Snow caused the closure of Interstate 5 over the Grapevine, and snow even fell on the Temblors and Diablo Range along the west side of the San Joaquin Valley. In Yosemite National Park and the surrounding area, winds toppled trees and downed power lines, and the combination|of heavy snow and rock slides closed roads into the Park. This was the first time since the floods of January, 1997, that Yosemite National Park was closed due to weather. Heavy snow and downed oak trees also plagued the El Portal and Wawona areas. Due to the power outages, Yosemite park officials evacuated some visitors to the park for a period of time due to the extensive impacts from this storm.||The morning of March 21st saw hail falling on the San Joaquin Valley floor, including near Rio Bravo and later over the Fresno-Clovis metro area. By the afternoon, hail was reported in Selma and Visalia, with ��-inch hail reported in the latter city. In the Tehachapi Mountains, up to a foot of new snow was reported.||The trough moved into the Great Basin, but a trailing short-wave brought light showers to the Kern County mountains and the far south end of the San Joaquin Valley during the early morning hours of March 22nd. Isolated showers were reported over parts of downtown Bakersfield, but stayed just south of Meadows Field.||Yet another storm reached the central California interior on March 23rd-24th. A line of thunderstorms developed on the 23rd over Merced County, then moved eastward into Madera and Mariposa Counties. Thunderstorms at the south end of the line propagated southward into Fresno County, ultimately reaching parts of Fresno and near Hanford. In fact, Fresno reported over a half inch of rain that day, with 0.38 inch falling in an hour at Fresno-Yosemite International Airport during the afternoon. Small hail, frequent lightning, and brief heavy rainfall were the main impacts of these storm cells. Funnel clouds were reported west of Madera, and near Clovis and Kerman; none touched down. The heavy rains caused flooding in the Southern Sierra Nevada foothills east of Fresno, as well as in downtown Fresno and southeast of Bakersfield. Convective cooling lowered the snow level down to 2200 feet in Mariposa and Madera Counties.||The last major storm of the month arrived on March 24th. This storm brought gusts to 45 mph to the west side of the San Joaquin Valley, and gusts to 65 mph in the Kern County mountains and deserts. Convective activity was limited to near Merced, with several reports of road flooding due to the already saturated ground. Thunderstorms and showers moved east into the foothills of Madera and Mariposa Counties, where the heavy rains triggered rock and mud slides. Mainly light showers occurred southward. The trough moved east of the region on the 25th, with residual light showers in its wake. Additional light snow fell in the Southern Sierra Nevada measuring around 5 inches or less. Local media reported that the roof of a vacant store at Shaver Lake collapsed on March 26th due to 6 feet of snow accumulation on the roof.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The CHP reported flooding on East Childs Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: February began dry and relatively cool due to a northerly flow aloft and an upper-level ridge anchored off the West Coast. Some patches of dense fog formed throughout the San Joaquin Valley during the evening of January 31st, but had dissipated by mid-morning on the 1st. Skies over the San Joaquin Valley were mostly clear, except for stratocumulus clouds banked against the foothills and lower elevations of the southern Sierra Nevada. High pressure remained off the coast through the 3rd, and the circulation around the high allowed cooler and drier air to filter into|central California from the Great Basin. This kept skies mostly clear throughout the central California interior.||On the 4th through the 7th, the offshore high pressure built onshore. Temperatures warmed to above normal values with mostly clear skies prevailing throughout the region. Temperatures reached the upper 60s to mid 70s in the San Joaquin Valley and the Kern County deserts on the 6th and 7th. A dry upper-level short-wave trough moved through California on February 6th. This short-wave brought gusty winds to the west side of the San Joaquin Valley and to the Indian Wells Valley, but had no impact on temperatures.
Slow moving low pressure dropped down the Pacific Northwest coast and into California bringing heavy snow to the Sierra and northeast California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cedarville CO-OP observer received 8 inches of snow from this system.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving low pressure dropped down the Pacific Northwest coast and into California bringing heavy snow to the Sierra and northeast California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 2.5 to nearly four feet were reported at the higher elevations around Lake Tahoe. In the Tahoe Basin, widespread snowfall totals of 14 to 27 inches fell. In addition to heavy snowfall, winds in the Tahoe Basin gusted to between 35 and 45 mph with a gust to 54 mph at 1134PST on the 25th at the Truckee Airport. Over the crest winds were gusting to 90 mph which caused the closure of Squaw Valley and Alpine Meadows ski areas. Finally, an avalanche near Donner Summit caused the closure of Interstate 80 on the 25th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving low pressure dropped down the Pacific Northwest coast and into California bringing heavy snow to the Sierra and northeast California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The bulk of the snow with this event fell west of highway 395. Twenty to 35 inches of snow fell near the crest and at the Mammoth and June Lake ski areas. Elsewhere, 13 inches fell at the Mammoth Ranger Station with an estimated 4 to 8 inches for much of the rest of county. In addition to snow, winds gusted to 35 to 45 mph in many areas with 50 to 60 mph in a few spots along and west of highway 395. Over the ridges, winds gusted as high as 134 mph at the Mammoth Mountain ski area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving low pressure dropped down the Pacific Northwest coast and into California bringing heavy snow to the Sierra and northeast California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hefty snowfall totals were reported especially from Susanville south and west. In that area, a widespread 15 to 30 inches of snow fell with local amounts to 38 inches (5 miles west of Beckwourth). Elsewhere, 8 to 12 inches of snow fell across far northwest Lassen County (around Beiber).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold winter storm dropped significant snowfall amounts over Northwest California.  This resulted in chain restrictions on the higher elevation roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several spotters reported snowfall meeting warning criteria.  Chain restrictions were also required on some of the main roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold winter storm dropped significant snowfall amounts over Northwest California.  This resulted in chain restrictions on the higher elevation roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several spotters reported snowfall meeting warning criteria.  Chain restrictions were also required on some of the main roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold winter storm dropped significant snowfall amounts over Northwest California.  This resulted in chain restrictions on the higher elevation roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several spotters reported snowfall meeting warning criteria.  Chain restrictions were also required on some of the main roads.

CZ_NAME: COASTAL NORTH BAY INCLUDING POINT REYES NATIONAL SEASHORE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a prolonged warm and dry spell a strong low pressure trough made its way out of the Gulf of Alaska and into California. This trough began the series of cool weather systems that produced six consecutive days of precipitation in the San Francisco Bay Area and the central California coast. Damaging wind, locally flooding rain, intense hail and even moderate snowfall above 1500 feet were experienced during the first wave of this series.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High tide combined with rain runoff to cause tidal flooding in Mill Valley. The Park and Ride at Highway 101 and Shoreline Highway was impacted by the high water. The high tide at Sausalito reached 6.4 feet around 9:33 a.m. PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system dug through southern California, bringing very strong winds, heavy rain and heavy snow, with blizzard conditions in the mountains. Several inches of rain were reported at favored upslope locations. The strongest winds occurred over the high desert where winds exceeded 110 mph at times, causing considerable damage in the Apple Valley area. Behind the front, scattered thunderstorms dropped more rain, periods of hail, and a few waterspouts, but causing only minor urban flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall began in most locations between 1500 and 1600 PST Sunday the 20th. A spotter in Wrightwood reported a storm total of 28 inches, with strong winds blowing all afternoon. A spotter in Running Springs reported a storm total of 20 inches. A spotter in Big Bear City reported a storm total of 15 inches at 6800 feet elevation, with a few inches more at the resorts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system dug through southern California, bringing very strong winds, heavy rain and heavy snow, with blizzard conditions in the mountains. Several inches of rain were reported at favored upslope locations. The strongest winds occurred over the high desert where winds exceeded 110 mph at times, causing considerable damage in the Apple Valley area. Behind the front, scattered thunderstorms dropped more rain, periods of hail, and a few waterspouts, but causing only minor urban flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds began at the Hesperia South mesonet station between 1153 and 1253 PST. High winds ended between 1253 and 1353 PST. During this time, a wind gust of 62 MPH was recorded.

In fact, Fresno reported over a half inch of rain that day, with 0.38 inch falling in an hour at Fresno-Yosemite International Airport during the afternoon. Small hail, frequent lightning, and brief heavy rainfall were the main impacts of these storm cells. Funnel clouds were reported west of Madera, and near Clovis and Kerman; none touched down. The heavy rains caused flooding in the Southern Sierra Nevada foothills east of Fresno, as well as in downtown Fresno and southeast of Bakersfield. Convective cooling lowered the snow level down to 2200 feet in Mariposa and Madera Counties.||The last major storm of the month arrived on March 24th. This storm brought gusts to 45 mph to the west side of the San Joaquin Valley, and gusts to 65 mph in the Kern County mountains and deserts. Convective activity was limited to near Merced, with several reports of road flooding due to the already saturated ground. Thunderstorms and showers moved east into the foothills of Madera and Mariposa Counties, where the heavy rains triggered rock and mud slides. Mainly light showers occurred southward. The trough moved east of the region on the 25th, with residual light showers in its wake. Additional light snow fell in the Southern Sierra Nevada measuring around 5 inches or less. Local media reported that the roof of a vacant store at Shaver Lake collapsed on March 26th due to 6 feet of snow accumulation on the roof.

The weather pattern then changed sharply, transitioning to a 12-day period which saw periods of heavy precipitation and below normal temperatures.||From the evening of the 15th until the afternoon of the 16th, a storm system passed over the area, bringing precipitation mainly to the mountains and foothills, but light amounts did fall on the San Joaquin Valley floor. Most of the precipitation fell mainly as rain over the Southern Sierra Nevada in the northern part of WFO San Joaquin Valley-Hanford���s forecast/warning area. The highest amounts, which occurred in Yosemite National Park, ranged from about 1.5 to 3 inches; this was the most precipitation that had occurred in the region for the month thus far. However, this was just the harbinger of what was to come only 4 days later. ||While the rain was focused toward the north, winds were the main concern over the southern part of the region. The winds pushed clouds over the San Joaquin Valley into the Tehachapi Mountains, creating areas of dense fog. Visibilities as low as 100 feet were reported near Tehachapi.||The next storm arrived two days later. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northerly offshore surface flow developed over the central California coast as a low pressure trough deepened in the desert southwest. This resulted in damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong and gusty northerly wind caused numerous trees and power lines to topple in Santa Cruz County. An acacia tree was knocked over at 10:10 a.m. PST on the 700 block of Amesti Road in Watsonville. A pine tree fell in the northbound lanes of Highway 1 just north of Park Avenue about 10:45 a.m. PST causing traffic to backup for more than a mile. Downed trees were also reported just after 11:00 a.m. PST at the 400 block of Roxas Street in Santa Cruz, the 800 block of 35th Avenue in Live Oak, and at Clares Street and Wharf Road in Capitola. The wind also snapped a power pole on Clares Street in Capitola.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a prolonged warm and dry spell a strong low pressure trough made its way out of the Gulf of Alaska and into California. This trough began the series of cool weather systems that produced six consecutive days of precipitation in the San Francisco Bay Area and the central California coast. Damaging wind, locally flooding rain, intense hail and even moderate snowfall above 1500 feet were experienced during the first wave of this series.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong and gusty wind developed ahead of a long wave trough. Southwest to west wind began to increase during the afternoon and peaked in the evening. A trained weather spotter reported a measured wind gust of 60 MPH at 6:15 p.m. PST. Other automated observation systems around the area above 1,000 feet in elevation reported gusts up to 83 MPH. The wind caused large trees and power lines to fall. Also, road closures occurred due to the downed trees and power lines. Of note, Porter Creek Road was closed until 3:00 p.m. Wednesday due to the efforts of removing a large tree that fell across the roadway. Overall, more than a dozen power poles were pushed down in Marin County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a prolonged warm and dry spell a strong low pressure trough made its way out of the Gulf of Alaska and into California. This trough began the series of cool weather systems that produced six consecutive days of precipitation in the San Francisco Bay Area and the central California coast. Damaging wind, locally flooding rain, intense hail and even moderate snowfall above 1500 feet were experienced during the first wave of this series.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong and gusty wind developed ahead of a long wave trough. Southwest to west wind began to increase late in the afternoon and peaked in the late evening. A mesonet automated weather reporting system measured a wind gust of 60 MPH at midnight. Other automated observation systems around the area above 1,000 feet in elevation reported gusts up to 83 MPH. The wind caused large trees and power lines to fall. Also, road closures occurred due to the downed trees and power lines. Of note, a tree fell onto a house on Alta Avenue in Piedmont. Overall, more than 6,500 customers lost power in the San Francisco Bay Area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a prolonged warm and dry spell a strong low pressure trough made its way out of the Gulf of Alaska and into California. This trough began the series of cool weather systems that produced six consecutive days of precipitation in the San Francisco Bay Area and the central California coast. Damaging wind, locally flooding rain, intense hail and even moderate snowfall above 1500 feet were experienced during the first wave of this series.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pre-dawn downpours caused localized small stream flooding in some areas in central Sonoma County. Those of note were Todd Road west of Stony Point Road outside of Santa Rosa, and Green Hill Road near Ross Road in Granton, where a man drove into the high water and became stuck for a time just after 7:00 a.m. PST. Flooding also caused the closure of Green Valley Road near Sullivan Road, where another vehicle stalled in the high water, and made a pond out of Shiloh Road near Windsor Road. Minor rock and mud slides were reported throughout the area between Santa Rosa and Calistoga.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a prolonged warm and dry spell a strong low pressure trough made its way out of the Gulf of Alaska and into California. This trough began the series of cool weather systems that produced six consecutive days of precipitation in the San Francisco Bay Area and the central California coast. Damaging wind, locally flooding rain, intense hail and even moderate snowfall above 1500 feet were experienced during the first wave of this series.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong and gusty wind knocked over an 80 foot tall Monterey pine tree onto two fifth-wheel trailers around 10:00 a.m. PST at the New Brighton State Beach campground. The trailers were destroyed and there were no injuries. More trees and also power lines were downed in and around Santa Cruz. At Santa Cruz and Centennial Streets around noon power lines fell causing power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: February began dry and relatively cool due to a northerly flow aloft and an upper-level ridge anchored off the West Coast. Some patches of dense fog formed throughout the San Joaquin Valley during the evening of January 31st, but had dissipated by mid-morning on the 1st. Skies over the San Joaquin Valley were mostly clear, except for stratocumulus clouds banked against the foothills and lower elevations of the southern Sierra Nevada. High pressure remained off the coast through the 3rd, and the circulation around the high allowed cooler and drier air to filter into|central California from the Great Basin. This kept skies mostly clear throughout the central California interior.||On the 4th through the 7th, the offshore high pressure built onshore. Temperatures warmed to above normal values with mostly clear skies prevailing throughout the region. Temperatures reached the upper 60s to mid 70s in the San Joaquin Valley and the Kern County deserts on the 6th and 7th. A dry upper-level short-wave trough moved through California on February 6th. This short-wave brought gusty winds to the west side of the San Joaquin Valley and to the Indian Wells Valley, but had no impact on temperatures.

CZ_NAME: COASTAL NORTH BAY INCLUDING POINT REYES NATIONAL SEASHORE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slight increase in swell caused higher than normal waves at the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 51 year-old woman walking with her husband at Portuguese Beach in Bodega Bay attempted to rescue their dog from the surf. During the rescue attempt the woman was caught in the surf herself and swept into the water. The waves were reported to be up to ten feet. Both the woman and her dog drowned.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a prolonged warm and dry spell a strong low pressure trough made its way out of the Gulf of Alaska and into California. This trough began the series of cool weather systems that produced six consecutive days of precipitation in the San Francisco Bay Area and the central California coast. Damaging wind, locally flooding rain, intense hail and even moderate snowfall above 1500 feet were experienced during the first wave of this series.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain led to small stream flooding and minor rock slides in Santa Cruz County. Near Riverdale Boulevard north of Boulder Creek, water washed over a bridge at Highway 9 around 8:40 a.m. PST. Around 2:00 p.m. PST a minor rock slide was reported near Laurel Glen and Soquel San Jose Roads. And, about 3:50 p.m. PST another rock slide occurred on Highway 129 at Rogge Lane near Watsonville, which stopped traffic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a prolonged warm and dry spell a strong low pressure trough made its way out of the Gulf of Alaska and into California. This trough began the series of cool weather systems that produced six consecutive days of precipitation in the San Francisco Bay Area and the central California coast. Damaging wind, locally flooding rain, intense hail and even moderate snowfall above 1500 feet were experienced during the first wave of this series.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds and rough seas combined to cause a 15-foot boat to capsize near Bodega Bay resulting in the death of a 65 year-old man.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a prolonged warm and dry spell a strong low pressure trough made its way out of the Gulf of Alaska and into California. This trough began the series of cool weather systems that produced six consecutive days of precipitation in the San Francisco Bay Area and the central California coast. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong and gusty wind caused trees and power lines to fall resulting in several hundred customers to lose power and about a half-dozen roads to close. Of significance was an oak tree that damaged a vehicle as it was knocked over by the wind around 6:30 p.m. PST on River Road near Trenton Healdsburg Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: February began dry and relatively cool due to a northerly flow aloft and an upper-level ridge anchored off the West Coast. Some patches of dense fog formed throughout the San Joaquin Valley during the evening of January 31st, but had dissipated by mid-morning on the 1st. Skies over the San Joaquin Valley were mostly clear, except for stratocumulus clouds banked against the foothills and lower elevations of the southern Sierra Nevada. High pressure remained off the coast through the 3rd, and the circulation around the high allowed cooler and drier air to filter into|central California from the Great Basin. This kept skies mostly clear throughout the central California interior.||On the 4th through the 7th, the offshore high pressure built onshore. Temperatures warmed to above normal values with mostly clear skies prevailing throughout the region. Temperatures reached the upper 60s to mid 70s in the San Joaquin Valley and the Kern County deserts on the 6th and 7th. A dry upper-level short-wave trough moved through California on February 6th. This short-wave brought gusty winds to the west side of the San Joaquin Valley and to the Indian Wells Valley, but had no impact on temperatures.

CZ_NAME: LOS ANGELES COUNTY MOUNTAINS EXCLUDING THE SANTA MONICA RANGE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong and gusty northerly winds developed across the Santa Ynez Range in Santa Barbara County and the Los Angeles County mountains. Sustained winds in excess of 40 MPH and gusts in excess of 65 MPH were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong north to northeast winds developed across the mountains of Los Angeles County. Mill Creek RAWS reported wind gusts between 58 and 60 MPH. the Chilao RAWS reported sustained winds 40-44 MPH with gusts 58-61 MPH. The Camp Nine RAWS sensor reported wind gusts between 58 and 71 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong and gusty northerly winds developed across the Santa Ynez Range in Santa Barbara County and the Los Angeles County mountains. Sustained winds in excess of 40 MPH and gusts in excess of 65 MPH were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northerly winds developed through the Santa Ynez Range. The Montecito RAWS sensor reported sustained winds between 40 and 48 MPH with gusts between 59 and 72 MPH.

By February 12th, satellite loops showed a series of storms over the eastern Pacific. Computer forecast models indicated that the storm forecast to arrive around the 17th would be a very cold system with snow levels dropping into the foothills.|The first cold front moved into the central California interior on the 14th, and bringing little precipitation, but noticeably cooler temperatures. There were gusty winds associated with the front with some locations reaching advisory levels along the west side of the San Joaquin Valley and parts of the Kern County deserts. This cold front set the stage for a stronger storm to arrive the late in the following day.|Strong winds developed across the region ahead of the second storm, beginning on the evening of February 15th and continuing into the next day. Bakersfield had gusts to 38 mph during the morning of the 16th, and gusts of 40-50 mph were reported both over the west side of the San Joaquin Valley and in the Kern County deserts. During the morning of the 16th, thunderstorms developed over the central Sierra Nevada along the cold front, and extended as far south as Yosemite National Park. The cold front rapidly moved through central California during the morning hours, with a cold, unstable airmass moving into the San Joaquin Valley behind it. There was some clearing after frontal passage, and this allowed for some solar heating. As a result, a line of thunderstorms developed over southwestern Fresno and northern Kings counties, moving east into northwestern Tulare County. There were several hail reports from these thunderstorms, with hail measuring up to a half-inch in diameter.|Significant rain and snow finally returned by the 16th, with storms impacting the area until the 19th of the month. The strongest storm reached the central California interior on the 18th, bringing heavy rain and snow, very strong winds and a deep pool of cold air; snow levels fell to around 2000 feet, well into the Southern Sierra Nevada foothills. Windy conditions occurred in the southern end of the San Joaquin Valley and Grapevine areas; for instance, a gust of 47 mph occurred at Bakersfield Meadows Field Airport on the 18th. A gust of 86 mph, with a sustained wind speed of 64 mph, occurred on Grapevine Peak during that evening. This wind blew down to|the base of the Grapevine, with wind gusts exceeding 70 mph at the California Highway Patrol weigh station at the south end of the San Joaquin Valley. These high winds even briefly hampered travel on Interstate 5 over the Grapevine and through Tejon Pass. The upper-level low associated with this storm remained off the central California Coast. The circulation around the low spun bands of rain northward over the western San Joaquin Valley. The persistent rains caused some street flooding between Coalinga and Fresno during the early morning hours of February 19th. Later that day, thunderstorms developed over the central California coast. A couple of these storms drifted over the Temblors into the west side of the San Joaquin Valley. One thunderstorm dropped hail up to a quarter inch in diameter on Lemoore.|Further east, up to 16 inches of new snow fell over the high country of the Southern Sierra Nevada, and spotters in the foothills reported up to 6 inches of snow.
A brief tornado touched down near the town of Maxwell in Colusa County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 3:05 pm  an EF0 tornado touched down in a pasture about 2 miles west of downtown Maxwell.  The pasture is located just north of Maxwell Sites Rd., between McDermott Rd. and Danley Rd.  There was no damage observed, but several witnesses observed a brief touchdown in the grassy field, which is used as pasture for a herd of buffalo.  There were no injuries nor fatalities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of unseasonably strong Pacific storms produced spotty high winds in the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak measured gust for this event was 63 mph 1 mile SSW of Independence.  A helicopter parked in Independence was tossed by the wind late in the evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado touched down near the town of Williams in Colusa County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 3:00 pm an EF0 tornado touched down about a mile WSW of downtown Williams in Colusa County, near the intersection of Hwy 20 and Davis Road.  The maximum wind speed of the tornado was estimated at 80 mph with a damage path of three quarters of a mile.  The damage path was in an east-northeast direction.  No injuries nor fatalities have been reported.  The tornado damaged six homes, an out-building, two vehicles, and a home radio tower.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down near Mather Industrial Park in Sacramento County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 3:05 pm an EF0 tornado touched down at the Mather Field Industrial Park, immediately north of Mather Field.  The maximum wind speed of the tornado was estimated at 75 mph with a damage path of one third of a mile.  The damage path was in a northeast direction.  No injuries nor fatalities have been reported.  Damage was to a few trees including a large evergreen tree, broken road signs, and broken windows to multiple cars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado touched down near Rockville in Solano County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 3:52 pm an EF0 tornado touched down less than a mile south of downtown Rockville in Solano County, near Rockville Road and Suisun Valley Road.  The maximum wind speed of the tornado was estimated at 70 mph with a damage path of 200 yards.  There were no injuries nor fatalities.  The tornado caused roof damage to two small businesses and damaged several trees, fences, and signs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough dropped out of the Gulf of Alaska and approached the California coast on April 6th. This feature brought cooler temperatures, with Fresno down 6 degrees from the 5th, and Bakersfield down 4 degrees. The trough moved over interior central California the next day, bringing gusty winds, scattered mainly afternoon and evening thunderstorms, and much colder temperatures. The high at Fresno on April 7th was only 55 degrees, down 20 degrees from the previous day. Bakersfield had a high of 59 degrees, down 18 degrees from the 6th. There were several reports of one quarter to one half inch hail on the San Joaquin Valley floor. Several inches of snow fell in the Southern Sierra Nevada foothills fell as low as the communities of Bootjack, Oakhurst and Granite Station. Nineteen inches of snow fell at Lodgepole; this was the highest amount reported from either the Southern Sierra Nevada or the Tehachapi Mountains. In the Kern County deserts, winds gusted to 45-55 mph on April 7th behind the cold front. The upper-level low associated with the trough moved to near Point Conception during the afternoon of April 8th. This shifted the focus of precipitation westward, to over the west side of the San Joaquin Valley. Even so, precipitation was mostly light. A deep, cold airmass filled the San Joaquin Valley, and the high temperatures at Bakersfield and Fresno on the 8th were the same as the previous day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway Patrol reported flooding on interstate 5 just north of Quebec Avenue.

A very cold, late-season storm system dropped south through California, bringing a wintry mix of rain and mountain snow, as well as strong winds. In advance of the storm, winds gusted to 60 mph east of the mountain crests, causing some downed power lines and trees. Due to the inside track of the storm, moisture was limited and confined in and west of the mountains. Some areas even saw some lightning and periods of small hail in the post-frontal, unstable air. Snow totals were as follows: 7 to 14 inches above 6500 feet, 3 to 6 inches above 5500 feet, 1 to 3 inches above 2800 feet and due to the very cold temperatures, the snow level was as low as 2000 feet with traces of snow there. The very low snow level is significant for the month of April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds were responsible for moderate damage in the Coachella Valley on the 7th. A power line fell in an alley in the town of Coachella, causing six homes to be evacuated as a precaution while power was restored. Two trees fell in the Indian Springs area, one landing on the hood of an SUV and the other blocking traffic in Palm Desert. No injuries or fires were reported with any of the incidents. The Palm Springs Tramway closed its mountain station early due to high winds.

Deepening low pressure aloft and strong onshore flow brought gusty winds to southern California, mainly in the mountains and deserts. These winds toppled several trees and at least one power pole, which resulted in a small brush fire in Palm Springs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy winds knocked over a power pole around 1530 PST on the 9th near Stevens Road and Via Monte Vista, according to the Palm Springs Fire Department. Winds were gusting in the middle 50s at many nearby sensors during the day. The power pole fell into a vacant lot where it started a small brush fire, which was contained within a half hour and did not spread beyond that lot. Power was out for around 400 customers in the area for about a day. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deepening low pressure aloft and strong onshore flow brought gusty winds to southern California, mainly in the mountains and deserts. These winds toppled several trees and at least one power pole, which resulted in a small brush fire in Palm Springs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds took down 35-40 trees at the Rancho Mirage Public library between late Sunday (8th) and early Monday (9th). The trees were mature, 15 to 20 foot tall mesquites and palo verdes. Nearby Thousand Palms recorded a wind gust of 40 MPH at 0300 PST Monday, while Thermal airport recorded a gust of 53 MPH at 2300 PST Sunday. No other damage was done to the library and no one was injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold storm system dropped south from the Gulf of Alaska, bringing waves of precipitation and a few thunderstorms with pea-sized hail to the area. Upslope flow helped to increase precipitation totals in the mountains in general, but the upper-level dynamics caused the highest totals in the San Diego County mountains and surrounding areas. Large flows occurred in the Tijuana River and its tributaries. The snow level dropped to 3500 feet toward the end of the event, resulting in well over a foot of snow at many of the mountain ski resorts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread urban flooding of highways and rural flooding of secondary streets in the Indio/La Quinta/Thermal area. Instantaneous rain rates were a quarter to a third of an inch per hour at times. There was also flash flooding of washes at the Whitewater River crossings (Dune Palms/Adams Street) in La Quinta.

CZ_NAME: LOS ANGELES COUNTY MOUNTAINS EXCLUDING THE SANTA MONICA RANGE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm brought significant rain and snow to Southwestern California. Rainfall totals for the storm generally ranged between 1 and 4 inches across the area with the highest rainfall amounts across San Luis Obispo County. Significant snowfall was reported across the mountains with 12 to 24 inches of snowfall above 6000 feet and 3 to 8 inches below 6000 feet. In addition, cold and unstable air associated with the storm generated some strong thunderstorms across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm brought winter storm conditions to the mountains of Los Angeles county. Snowfall totals between 12 and 24 inches were reported above 6000 feet while amounts between 3 and 8 inches were reported between 3500 and 6000 feet. Along with the snowfall, strong southerly winds with gusts between 60 and 70 MPH were reported in some spots. The combination of heavy snow and gusty winds produced very hazardous winter storm conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold Pacific storm brought locally heavy low elevation snow to the Mojave Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 12 inches of snow fell on Townes Pass (4100') on Highway 190. Several hours later, 3 inches fell on the desert floor at Tecopa (1300').

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold storm system dropped south from the Gulf of Alaska, bringing waves of precipitation and a few thunderstorms with pea-sized hail to the area. Upslope flow helped to increase precipitation totals in the mountains in general, but the upper-level dynamics caused the highest totals in the San Diego County mountains and surrounding areas. Large flows occurred in the Tijuana River and its tributaries. The snow level dropped to 3500 feet toward the end of the event, resulting in well over a foot of snow at many of the mountain ski resorts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds began at Heaps Peak between 1915 and 2015 PST, during which time a peak gust of 60 MPH was measured by the Heaps Peak RAWS. High winds ended between 2315 and 0015 (on the 19th) PST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm brought winter storm conditions to the mountains of Santa Barbara County. Snowfall totals between 6 and 14 inches were reported across the higher elevations of the Santa Barbara County mountains. Along with the snowfall, strong southerly winds with gusts between 60 and 70 MPH were reported in some spots. The combination of heavy snow and gusty winds produced very hazardous winter storm conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm brought significant rain and snow to Southwestern California. Rainfall totals for the storm generally ranged between 1 and 4 inches across the area with the highest rainfall amounts across San Luis Obispo County. Significant snowfall was reported across the mountains with 12 to 24 inches of snowfall above 6000 feet and 3 to 8 inches below 6000 feet. In addition, cold and unstable air associated with the storm generated some strong thunderstorms across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm brought winter storm conditions to the mountains of Ventura County. Snowfall totals between 12 and 24 inches were reported above 6000 feet while amounts between 3 and 8 inches were reported between 3500 and 6000 feet. Along with the snowfall, strong southerly winds with gusts between 60 and 70 MPH were reported in some spots. The combination of heavy snow and gusty winds produced very hazardous winter storm conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread freezing temperatures continued into December 8th across the San Joaquin Valley. Seven consecutive days of freezing temperatures took it's toll on the citrus crop in the San Joaquin Valley.  ||According to Citrus Growers' Association California Citrus Mutual, about 35 percent of the mandarin orange crop was lost to frost and freeze damage. The news was a little better for the navel crop, about 15 percent were not expected to make it. Initial estimates were not expected to be severe, however after several weeks of inspecting the citrus crop, it was determined that damages were much worse than first expected.||87 million dollars was spent to protect crops from the extended frost and freeze.

A cold storm system dropped south from the Gulf of Alaska, bringing waves of precipitation and a few thunderstorms with pea-sized hail to the area. Upslope flow helped to increase precipitation totals in the mountains in general, but the upper-level dynamics caused the highest totals in the San Diego County mountains and surrounding areas. Large flows occurred in the Tijuana River and its tributaries. The snow level dropped to 3500 feet toward the end of the event, resulting in well over a foot of snow at many of the mountain ski resorts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds began in Fremont Canyon between 1830 and 1930 PST, during which time a peak gust of 61 MPH was measured by the Fremont Canyon RAWS. High winds ended between 2030 and 2130 PST.

Rainfall totals for the storm generally ranged between 1 and 4 inches across the area with the highest rainfall amounts across San Luis Obispo County.
