EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm winds developed across Vandenberg Air Force Base. The ASOS on the base reported wind gusts to 64 MPH in association with the severe thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The next storm moved into California on December 15th. Rain fell as far south as Madera on the east side of the San Joaquin Valley, but fell along the length of the west side of the Valley with light rain falling as far south as Bakersfield. This case of rain shadowing resulted in Fresno remaining dry while measurable rain fell to the north, west and south of the city. The trace of rain that fell at Meadows Field on December 15th-16th was the only rain for Bakersfield for the month of December, tying for the third driest December on record.||Showers continued over the Kern County deserts into the 17th. A couple of evening showers drifted northwest across the Tehachapi Mountains, bringing light rain as far north as Wheeler Ridge. Another system dropped south along the California coast the next day, moving inland on December 19th. Although this system only brought some clouds to the state, it was the ridge that developed behind the trough that brought extreme cold to the central and southern San Joaquin Valley.||The Tule fog returned to the San Joaquin Valley after the trough pushed east of the state. Fog development occurred for the next several days over the San Joaquin Valley; while the higher elevations, including the Sierra Nevada, nearby foothills, and the desert areas, generally had plenty of sunshine. An inside-slider trough moved over the Great Basin and allowed the upper-level ridge to weaken by the 21st of the month.

CZ_NAME: SAN BERNARDINO AND RIVERSIDE COUNTY VALLEYS - THE INLAND EMPIRE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold upper level low pressure system moved southward from the Four Corners region into northern Mexico. Strong surface high pressure settled over the Great Basin and helped to bring strong offshore winds from the 2nd through the 4th of the month. The strongest winds occurred in and below the canyons and passes, causing downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds knocked over a 70-75' tree with a 30 diameter trunk that fell on three units of a multi-unit apartment community in Glen Avon, near Riverside. Power lines were also down. No injuries reported, but the three units were declared uninhabitable and the residents relocated.|Five big rigs went down due to winds in the early morning on the 3rd in the area near Interstates 10 and 15 interchange in Ontario. Another crashed on HIghway 60 near I-15. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold storm system dropped south from the Gulf of Alaska, accompanied by a strong cold front packing gusty winds and moderate to heavy rain and snow. Snow fell as low as 1000 feet, with accumulations generally above 1500 feet. Winter Storm conditions developed in the mountains above 5000 feet where 15 to 30 inches of snow was reported by the end of the storm. Gusty winds also contributed to areas of blowing snow, further reducing visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter at the Palomar Observatory reported a storm total snowfall of 16 inches at 5600 feet. A spotter on Mt. Laguna reported a storm total snowfall of 23 inches. Spotters down to 3000 feet reported 3-6 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure trough moved into California on November 10th. Ahead of the trough, a strong offshore flow brought southeast winds to the region. As these winds downsloped through the Tejon and Tehachapi Passes, very warm and above-normal temperatures occurred in the south end of the San Joaquin Valley. Bakersfield had a high of 78 on November 10th, and 76 the next day. Winds gusted at the base of the Grapevine as well, reaching 61 mph during the afternoon of November 10th, and 73 mph before sunrise the following morning.||This storm also brought snow across the Southern Sierra and Kern County Mountains. Snowfall reports included 7 inches of new snow at Ponderosa and 2th. Snow fell at Pine Mountain Club (at 6100 feet in the western Tehachapi Mountains) during the evening of November 11th, and winds gusted to 56 mph at the base of the Grapevine during the morning g hours of the 12th. A high pressure ridge built over the east Pacific, setting up a dry northwest wind flow over California.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold system dropping south from the Gulf of Alaska pushed a powerful cold front through the region with strong winds followed by heavy snow for the Sierra and northeast California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOTEL data and reports from the Mammoth Mountain ski area and spotters indicate that 13 to 18 inches of snow fell near the crest and at higher elevations north of Bridgeport, much of it within 12 hours. Elsewhere, much less snow fell with only 6 to 10 inches of snow near and east of highway 395 from Bridgeport south to near Mammoth Lakes. In addition to snowfall, winds gusted to between 25 and 35 mph during the storm with local areas as high as 50 mph. Meanwhile, the Sierra crest gusted over 130 mph with 156 mph reported at the top of Mammoth Mountain at 0700PST on the 16th.

Finally, UPS deliveries were interrupted on the 15th due to the danger of delivery trucks being blown over.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Calistoga, CA emergency officials responded to nine calls between 2200 and 2300PST for wind damage. Damage included fallen trees, large tree limbs, and a metal awning ripped from a home in Chateau Calistoga Mobile Home Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong surface high pressure built into the Northwest States late on November 30 and into December 1 as an upper low moved south through Nevada and southeastern California. The surface high combined with strong northeast flow aloft over the Sierra to bring very strong winds near the crest with gusty winds below the crest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread winds gusting to between 40 and 50 mph from the east and northeast caused numerous trees to fall across power lines and into a few residences in South Lake Tahoe, especially near Pioneer Trail Blvd. (where trees were reported in the road). In addition, strong winds generated waves to several feet on Lake Tahoe which caused extensive damage to the Camp Richardson dock in South Lake Tahoe.

CZ_NAME: SANTA LUCIA MOUNTAINS AND LOS PADRES NATIONAL FOREST
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wind event from November 30, 2011 to December 2, 2011 caused damage and one fatality across the San Francisco and Monterey Bay areas. The fatality occurred on November 30, 2011. Wind damage occurred from November 30, 2011 through December 2, 2011.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 35 year-old male wildlife biologist of Big Sur was killed by a falling tree during high winds. The tree toppled over at 1450PST Wednesday, November 30, 2011 near North Coast Road at mile marker 16.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third in a series of winter snow storms struck northern California on this date. The snow levels were a bit higher with this storm and the only populated area that saw significant snow was the Mount Shasta City area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter S Tennant reported 12.0 inches of snow overnight and in the morning ending at 23/1100 PST.  A spotter N Tennant reported 12.0 inches of snow in 19 hours ending at 23/1800 PST, then another 7.5 inches of snow in 7.5 hours ending at 25/0630 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system from the Gulf of Alaska affected the San Francisco and Monterey Bay areas from January 1st through January 4th. This system brought strong gusty winds and heavy rain to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 3:30 a.m. PST on January 1, 2011 State Route 29 was closed from St. Helena (Napa County) to the Napa/Lake County line due to snow. The roadway reopened at 7:12 a.m. PST on January 1, 2011.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system from the Gulf of Alaska affected the San Francisco and Monterey Bay areas from January 1st through January 4th. This system brought strong gusty winds and heavy rain to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree fell on an occupied vehicle causing the closure of State Route 183 in the Monterey Area at approximately 9:21 p.m. PST. The roadway was reopened at 10:57 p.m.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system from the Gulf of Alaska affected the San Francisco and Monterey Bay areas from January 1st through January 4th. This system brought strong gusty winds and heavy rain to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 5:00 p.m. PST, State Route 9 at Scenic Way, in the Santa Cruz area, was closed due to downed trees and power lines. The roadway reopened on January 3, 2011, at approximately 6:21 a.m.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system from the Gulf of Alaska affected the San Francisco and Monterey Bay areas from January 1st through January 4th. This system brought strong gusty winds and heavy rain to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 5:04 p.m. PST State Route 9 was closed from 1.2 miles north of Felton to 2.4 miles north of Felton due to downed power lines. The roadway reopened at 5:56 a.m. on January 3, 2011.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system from the Gulf of Alaska affected the San Francisco and Monterey Bay areas from January 1st through January 4th. This system brought strong gusty winds and heavy rain to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Black ice made rural roads treacherous, including Porter Creek Road, where a car slid, flipped and finally smashed into a bedroom wall of a home. There were two other accidents due to black ice on roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific Storm brought high winds and high surf to Northern and Central California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thirty year old male big-wave surfer was injured attempting to surf at Mavericks, a well known big wave surf area 25 miles south of San Francisco near Half Moon Bay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific Storm brought high winds and high surf to Northern and Central California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree felled by high winds crushed a car in Oakland during the early morning hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third in a series of winter snow storms struck northern California on this date. The snow levels were a bit higher with this storm and the only populated area that saw significant snow was the Mount Shasta City area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow levels stayed above populated areas in CAZ080 during this storm, but based on numerous heavy snow reports in adjacent CAZ082 and heavy rain reported in CAZ080, it is assumed that heavy snow fell at higher elevations in CAZ080. 3.96 inches of rain in 48 hours ending at 25/0400 PST fell at the Scott Mountain Near Callahan RAWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third in a series of winter snow storms struck northern California on this date. The snow levels were a bit higher with this storm and the only populated area that saw significant snow was the Mount Shasta City area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cooperative observer at Mt. Shasta City reported 21.0 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 24/1900 PST, then another 15.0 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 25/1900 PST. A spotter SSE Mount Shasta reported 7.0 inches of snow in 8.5 hours ending at 24/1030 PST, then another 5.5 inches in 4.25 hours ending at 24/1445 PST, then another 5.0 inches in 5.5 hours ending at 24/2030 PST, then another 8.5 inches in 11 hours ending at 25/0730 PST for a grand total of 26.0 inches.  A spotter 6ENE Mount Shasta reported 8.0 inches of snow overnight ending at 23/1200 PST.  A spotter 6NW Mount Shasta reported 8.0 inches of snow in 11 hours ending at 24/0700 PST.  A spotter 4SW Weed reported 14.0 inches of snow in 17 hours ending at 24/0700 PST, then another 10.5 inches of snow in 6 hours ending at 24/1300 PST, then another 7.0 inches of snow in 18 hours ending at 25/0700 PST for a grand total of 31.5 inches.  A spotter 1W Mount Shasta reported 12.0 inches of snow in 12 hours ending at 24/1000 PST.  A spotter ESE Mount Shasta reported 12.0 inches of snow in 12 hours ending at 24/1100 PST.  A spotter 1W Mount Shasta reported 24.0 inches of snow in 21 hours ending at 25/0700 PST.  A spotter 6NW Mount Shasta reported 10.0 inches of snow in 12 hours ending at 25/0800 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The new year (and decade) began with the latest in a series of Pacific storms that stretched back to December 17th, 2010. This storm brought strong, gusty winds to the region, especially to the base of the Grapevine, where gusts up to 89 mph were recorded on January 2nd. Snow fell on both the Grapevine and Tehachapi Pass, closing both routes between the San Joaquin Valley and southern California. Unlike the storms in December, this storm was focused more on Fresno, Kings, and Tulare counties, Fresno recorded 1.51 inch of rain on January 1st-2nd, while Bakersfield only received 0.16 inch. This was a cold storm, with the snow level falling to around 2500 feet. With the San Joaquin Valley floor and the lower foothills saturated from the record rains of the latter half of December, flood watches were posted for the New Year���s Day holiday weekend.||As the night of January 1st-2nd progressed, wind gusts to 50 mph were recorded in the far south end of the San Joaquin Valley, at the base of the Grapevine. Over an inch of snow had fallen in Squirrel Valley--at 2700 feet in the hills above Lake Isabella--by 9:45 PM. The winds at the base of the Grapevine continued through the 2nd, with a peak wind of 89 mph recorded during the afternoon. Several roads on the San Joaquin Valley floor flooded due to runoff from locally heavy rains onto the saturated ground. In the Tehachapi Mountains, heavy snow closed Interstate 5 over the Grapevine (a total of 8 inches of snow fell at Lebec), and 52 cars were stuck on Highway 168 in the Southern Sierra Nevada at the 7000 foot level near Kaiser Point. Snow even fell in the Kern County deserts, with Ridgecrest reporting accumulating snow and snow falling at Edwards Air Force Base. The CHP reported numerous vehicles stuck on ice-covered southbound Hwy 395 just south of the junction with Hwy 178 and on northbound Hwy 395 just south of Ridgecrest Boulevard. A trained weather spotter in Ridgecrest measured 4 inches of new snow. The fire department in Mojave measured 4 inches. The fire department in California City measured 7 inches. The fire department in Randsburg measured 5 inches of the white stuff. The CHP reported vehicle stuck in the snow on Garlock Road just east of southbound Hwy 395.||The upper-level low moved south to off Point Conception on January 3rd. This kept a southerly flow over the Tehachapi Mountains, with advisory-level winds continuing at the base of the Grapevine. Although the precipitation had mostly ended, lingering runoff continued to create flooding hazards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water up to 3 feet deep on roads near Santa Lucia Drive and Manresa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The new year (and decade) began with the latest in a series of Pacific storms that stretched back to December 17th, 2010. This storm brought strong, gusty winds to the region, especially to the base of the Grapevine, where gusts up to 89 mph were recorded on January 2nd. Snow fell on both the Grapevine and Tehachapi Pass, closing both routes between the San Joaquin Valley and southern California. Unlike the storms in December, this storm was focused more on Fresno, Kings, and Tulare counties, Fresno recorded 1.51 inch of rain on January 1st-2nd, while Bakersfield only received 0.16 inch. This was a cold storm, with the snow level falling to around 2500 feet. With the San Joaquin Valley floor and the lower foothills saturated from the record rains of the latter half of December, flood watches were posted for the New Year���s Day holiday weekend.||As the night of January 1st-2nd progressed, wind gusts to 50 mph were recorded in the far south end of the San Joaquin Valley, at the base of the Grapevine. Over an inch of snow had fallen in Squirrel Valley--at 2700 feet in the hills above Lake Isabella--by 9:45 PM. The winds at the base of the Grapevine continued through the 2nd, with a peak wind of 89 mph recorded during the afternoon. Several roads on the San Joaquin Valley floor flooded due to runoff from locally heavy rains onto the saturated ground. In the Tehachapi Mountains, heavy snow closed Interstate 5 over the Grapevine (a total of 8 inches of snow fell at Lebec), and 52 cars were stuck on Highway 168 in the Southern Sierra Nevada at the 7000 foot level near Kaiser Point. Snow even fell in the Kern County deserts, with Ridgecrest reporting accumulating snow and snow falling at Edwards Air Force Base. The CHP reported numerous vehicles stuck on ice-covered southbound Hwy 395 just south of the junction with Hwy 178 and on northbound Hwy 395 just south of Ridgecrest Boulevard. A trained weather spotter in Ridgecrest measured 4 inches of new snow. The fire department in Mojave measured 4 inches. The fire department in California City measured 7 inches. The fire department in Randsburg measured 5 inches of the white stuff. The CHP reported vehicle stuck in the snow on Garlock Road just east of southbound Hwy 395.||The upper-level low moved south to off Point Conception on January 3rd. This kept a southerly flow over the Tehachapi Mountains, with advisory-level winds continuing at the base of the Grapevine. Although the precipitation had mostly ended, lingering runoff continued to create flooding hazards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CHP reported State Route 269 closed between State Route 198 and Huron due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first in a series of winter snow storms struck northern California on this date.  The snow levels mostly stayed above populated areas and affected passes, but the Mount Shasta City area was an exception to this rule.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cooperative observer at Mt. Shasta City reported 24.0 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 18/1900 PST, but it appears most of the snow fell the previous night into the morning.  A spotter 6NW Mount Shasta reported 10.0 inches of snow in 12 hours ending at 18/0530 PST. A spotter 4SW Weed reported 18.0 inches of snow in 16 hours ending at 18/0700 PST, then another 6 inches in 3 hours ending at 18/1000 PST.  A spotter 2SW Mount Shasta reported 6.0 inches in nearly 5 hours ending at 18/1150 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure trough moved into California on November 24th. This system brought some light rain to the region, but the main impact was from the mid and high clouds ahead of the cold front. These clouds inhibited fog development, resulting in a mostly fog-free Thanksgiving Day morning. Dense fog returned the next morning, mainly over the east side of the San Joaquin Valley and persisting into the late morning hours. The dense night and morning fog became more widespread over the central and southern San Joaquin Valley from November 26th through the 28th as a stagnant weather pattern lingered over central California. ||A strong upper-level trough approached the central California interior on November 29th, destabilizing the airmass and reducing the San Joaquin Valley fog. A tight surface pressure gradient developed over California, resulting in northeast winds gusting over 60 mph across the Southern Sierra Nevada. High winds developed on November 30th which is a relatively rare phenomenon known as a Mono Wind Event across the Sierra Nevada. This event continued into the first two days of December and caused extensive tree damage across much of the Southern Sierra Nevada. Please see the December, 2011 storm data report for more detailed storm reports from this event! ||At the lower elevations, a cold front moved through the San Joaquin Valley on November 30th, bringing northwest winds gusting to near 50 mph. The front also triggered high winds over the Kern County deserts, with gusts between 55 to 75 mph across the Inyokern area. Local media reported roof damage to a home in Inyokern and some trees and powerlines downed. Mountain waves developed over the south end of the Sierra Nevada during the evening of November 30th. One wave touched down near The Walker Pass, with the Blue Max weather station reporting a wind gust to 88 mph.

This is the continuation of the Mono wind event from November 30th.  This continued through December 2nd.||December began with an upper-level trough over the region and a low pressure center over the Desert Southwest. Northeast winds flowed around the low and knocked over numerous power line poles and trees throughout the central San Joaquin Valley, the Sierra Nevada foothills and Yosemite National Park. Fresno was also hit hard by power outages due to downed poles; this prompted a local emergency in the city during the afternoon of the 1st. Fresno County reported about $2 million in damages due to the strong winds. Mariposa County and especially near the town of Mariposa, numerous large trees were toppled by the high winds. Numerous large trees were downed in Yosemite National Park, with power outages and blocked roads in Yosemite Valley. ||Winds gusted to 45 mph at Fresno-Yosemite International Airport on December 1st, just shy of the record gust for December of 48 mph���on December 28th, 1991. Winds gusted to 60 mph at Tioga Pass, and over 50 mph at Coalinga. Trees were blown down at several locations, including Clovis and Mariposa. ||Officials at Devils Postpile National Monument in eastern Madera County reported widespread forest destruction in the monument with numerous large trees downed.  In addition roads were closed for months and several outbuildings were also damaged. ||An automated station at the summit of Mammoth Mountain a few miles to the east of Devils Postpile recorded sustained wind speed of 150 mph, and gusts in excess of 150 mph (the limit of the anemometer). Therefore wind gusts with this storm were estimated around 200 mph at the peak of the event early on December 1st. Red's Meadow in Devils Postpile is aligned in a NNE to SSW direction which favored the extreme downslope wind pattern from the nearby Sierra Peaks during this storm. The historic magnitude and duration of this event was caused primarily by an extreme pressure gradient set up between strong low pressure in the desert SW and strong high pressure off the Pacific NW coast.   ||This high wind event was known as a severe and relatively rare Mono Wind event; where winds flow over the Sierra Nevada and down into the foothills from the east instead of the typical westerly direction. These winds allow the air to warm adiabatically; temperatures rose dramatically in much of the San Joaquin Valley compared to the previous day.||Yet despite the severe winds, the southeastern part of the San Joaquin Valley remained almost completely wind sheltered and areas of dense fog developed during the night of November 30th-December 1st along a corridor stretching from Visalia south to Bakersfield.

The front also triggered high winds over the Kern County deserts, with gusts between 55 to 75 mph across the Inyokern area. Local media reported roof damage to a home in Inyokern and some trees and powerlines downed. Mountain waves developed over the south end of the Sierra Nevada during the evening of November 30th. One wave touched down near The Walker Pass, with the Blue Max weather station reporting a wind gust to 88 mph.

A low pressure trough and associated front moved through southern California each morning of the 17th and 18th. Rainfall was fairly significant for these systems, bringing around a half inch of rain to areas west of the mountains and up to an inch on some coastal slopes. Light snow even fell at the highest mountain elevations above 5000 feet. Strong onshore flow with gusty winds also accompanied these storms, however there were only a few gusts that reached advisory criteria in the mid-50s MPH, and no damage was reported. With the frontal passages, however, came record low max and min temperatures for numerous Southern California stations, such as Oceanside, Palomar Mountain and El Cajon.  A few single day precipitation records were also set.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On each of the 17th and 18th, numerous record low temperatures (both max and min) were set in the Coachella Valley. Palm Springs and Thermal both broke records on May 18, previously set in 1994 with high temperatures of 76 and 77 respectively. The longest record broken was for Indio on the 18th with a high temperature of 74 breaking the previous record of 77 set back in 1948. The temperatures seen in this area during these few days were also about 25-20 degrees below normal for that time of year.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much above average snowpack in the Sierra combined with warm to very warm temperatures to cause small stream flooding in Mono County late in June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A bridge crossing McGee Creek was destroyed by flood waters. The location of the washed out bridge is approximate.

This is the continuation of the Mono wind event from November 30th.  This continued through December 2nd.||December began with an upper-level trough over the region and a low pressure center over the Desert Southwest. Northeast winds flowed around the low and knocked over numerous power line poles and trees throughout the central San Joaquin Valley, the Sierra Nevada foothills and Yosemite National Park. Fresno was also hit hard by power outages due to downed poles; this prompted a local emergency in the city during the afternoon of the 1st. Fresno County reported about $2 million in damages due to the strong winds. Mariposa County and especially near the town of Mariposa, numerous large trees were toppled by the high winds. Numerous large trees were downed in Yosemite National Park, with power outages and blocked roads in Yosemite Valley. ||Winds gusted to 45 mph at Fresno-Yosemite International Airport on December 1st, just shy of the record gust for December of 48 mph���on December 28th, 1991. Winds gusted to 60 mph at Tioga Pass, and over 50 mph at Coalinga. Trees were blown down at several locations, including Clovis and Mariposa. ||Officials at Devils Postpile National Monument in eastern Madera County reported widespread forest destruction in the monument with numerous large trees downed.  In addition roads were closed for months and several outbuildings were also damaged. ||An automated station at the summit of Mammoth Mountain a few miles to the east of Devils Postpile recorded sustained wind speed of 150 mph, and gusts in excess of 150 mph (the limit of the anemometer). Therefore wind gusts with this storm were estimated around 200 mph at the peak of the event early on December 1st. Red's Meadow in Devils Postpile is aligned in a NNE to SSW direction which favored the extreme downslope wind pattern from the nearby Sierra Peaks during this storm. The historic magnitude and duration of this event was caused primarily by an extreme pressure gradient set up between strong low pressure in the desert SW and strong high pressure off the Pacific NW coast.   ||This high wind event was known as a severe and relatively rare Mono Wind event; where winds flow over the Sierra Nevada and down into the foothills from the east instead of the typical westerly direction. These winds allow the air to warm adiabatically; temperatures rose dramatically in much of the San Joaquin Valley compared to the previous day.||Yet despite the severe winds, the southeastern part of the San Joaquin Valley remained almost completely wind sheltered and areas of dense fog developed during the night of November 30th-December 1st along a corridor stretching from Visalia south to Bakersfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of systems affected the San Francisco and Monterey Bay areas with heavy rain and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In San Anselmo heavy rain and strong wind caused mudslides, trees to blow down and power poles to topple. The following are other damage reports from around the city. On Florence Road trees fell into the public right of way blocking the roadway and crushing a car. It also took down several PGE, cable and telephone poles. On Woodruff Ave, a mudslide traveled 1/4 mile down the road carrying structure debris and blocking a main intersection that accesses 12 homes.  On Scenic Ave, a mudslide entered a roadway and wind blew down a few power poles. At 29 Summit Ave, a mudslide closed roads; caused water, gas and sewer lines to break and took out numerous trees. On Redwood Rd, the road was damaged and even portions of the road collapse due to excess rain washing down hill. The roadway was closed between 390 and 405 Redwood Rd due to mudslide undermining the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At 10:46 pm on March 10, 2011, a magnitude 8.9 earthquake struck near the coast of Honshu, Japan. The earthquake generated a tsunami significantly affecting California on March 11, 2011.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tsunami impacted Santa Cruz...in particular Santa Cruz Harbor. Impacts included: eighteen vessels sunk, four vessels capsized, 100+ vessels with either superficial damage or waiting to be assessed, oil sheen on the water and serious damage to harbor facilities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At 10:46 pm on March 10, 2011, a magnitude 8.9 earthquake struck near the coast of Honshu, Japan. The earthquake generated a tsunami significantly affecting California on March 11, 2011.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tsunami affected the San Mateo County coast. A preliminary damage assessment was conducted due to the response, but no physical damage reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At 10:46 pm on March 10, 2011, a magnitude 8.9 earthquake struck near the coast of Honshu, Japan. The earthquake generated a tsunami significantly affecting California on March 11, 2011.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Berkeley Marina the tsunami damaged six boat slips, three docks, and snapped a wooden piling.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of systems affected the San Francisco and Monterey Bay areas with heavy rain and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds sheared off more than a dozen trees along a short stretch of Tomales-Petaluma Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of systems affected the San Francisco and Monterey Bay areas with heavy rain and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain overwhelmed a drainage pipe that runs underneath Pacific Cove Mobile Home Park, and then cascaded down Riverview Drive into the village causing damage to homes and businesses.

CZ_NAME: SOUTHERN MONTEREY BAY AND BIG SUR COAST
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of systems affected the San Francisco and Monterey Bay areas with heavy rain and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rock and mud slide closed Highway 1 south of Big Sur, just south of Limekiln Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of systems affected the San Francisco and Monterey Bay areas with heavy rain and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree fell and cleaved a house in two on the 400 block of Nelson Road outside Scotts Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of systems affected the San Francisco and Monterey Bay areas with heavy rain and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 60 to 70 foot Fir tree crashed into the roof, punching holes through the ceiling.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system dug through southern California, bringing very strong winds, heavy rain and heavy snow, with blizzard conditions in the mountains. Several inches of rain were reported at favored upslope locations. The strongest winds occurred over the high desert where winds exceeded 110 mph at times, causing considerable damage in the Apple Valley area. Behind the front, scattered thunderstorms dropped more rain, periods of hail, and a few waterspouts, but causing only minor urban flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter measured a gust of 115 MPH on an anemometer. The gust damaged the anemometer shortly after. Several power poles and trees were knocked down, causing a blackout that lasted nearly 24 hours. Numerous houses sustained roof damage and at lease one house had its roof destroyed. No related injuries were reported from the event.|Another spotter reported a measured wind gust of 59 MPH with sustained winds of 30 MPH, resulting in downed power lines, tiles blown off roof and power outages.|Yet another spotter measured sustained winds of 40 MPH with a gust of 65 MPH between 1300 and 1400 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system dug through southern California, bringing very strong winds, heavy rain and heavy snow, with blizzard conditions in the mountains. Several inches of rain were reported at favored upslope locations. The strongest winds occurred over the high desert where winds exceeded 110 mph at times, causing considerable damage in the Apple Valley area. Behind the front, scattered thunderstorms dropped more rain, periods of hail, and a few waterspouts, but causing only minor urban flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds began at the Burns Canyon RAWS around 0400 PST and ended around 1600 PST on the 20th. Numerous gusts over 80 MPH were recorded between 0500 and 1300 PST. A peak wind gust of 110 MPH was measured around 1030 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system dug through southern California, bringing very strong winds, heavy rain and heavy snow, with blizzard conditions in the mountains. Several inches of rain were reported at favored upslope locations. The strongest winds occurred over the high desert where winds exceeded 110 mph at times, causing considerable damage in the Apple Valley area. Behind the front, scattered thunderstorms dropped more rain, periods of hail, and a few waterspouts, but causing only minor urban flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 52 knots was measured by a Huntington Beach lifeguard between 1315 and 1415 PST on the 20th. He also reported the wind blowing sand and trash cans around the beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually cold air mass combined with clearing skies to bring cold low temperatures to the area on this morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The only reported low temperature from this zone was 24 degrees at Callahan. Adjacent zone CAZ081 reported low temperatures ranging from 20 to 27 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure trough brought heavy rain to the area which led to flooding in Sonoma County. Strong orthogonal winds enhanced the precipitation over the mountainous areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Green Valley Road, west of Sullivan in the town of Graton was closed due to flooding. Mark West Station Road, at Starr Road in the town of Windsor was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several areas of cold low pressure brought several rounds of snow and gusty winds to eastern California from early morning on the 23rd through the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of snow occurred during this storm, the first on the 23rd and the stronger second round on the 24th. The first round dropped the heaviest snow near the crest where 12 to 20 inches of snow was reported, with other areas only receiving 3 to 6 inches. The second round dropped 2 to 4 feet above 7000 feet and 1 to 2 feet elsewhere.  In addition to snowfall, gusty winds with the second round of snow caused low visibilities and the closure of Interstate 80 from Truckee to Colfax. Also, highway 89 was closed for avalanche control on the 24th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of weather systems bringing heavy rain, strong winds, high surf, and a tornado affected the District on March 16-21, 2011. Two deaths occurred during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado completely destroyed a metal building and severely bent a metal gate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of weather systems bringing heavy rain, strong winds, high surf, and a tornado affected the District on March 16-21, 2011. Two deaths occurred during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 35 year old welder from Hawaii died around 6 pm Wednesday in high surf. The wave faces reached heights of 40 to 45 feet on the biggest sets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of weather systems bringing heavy rain, strong winds, high surf, and a tornado affected the District on March 16-21, 2011. Two deaths occurred during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain on saturated soils caused a large landslide on State Route One 13.7 miles north of Big Sur State Park at Rocky Creek. The roadway reopened to one-way controlled traffic on April 20, 2011.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of weather systems bringing heavy rain, strong winds, high surf, and a tornado affected the District on March 16-21, 2011. Two deaths occurred during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports indicate that as many as fourteen sailboats and one houseboat floated free in Richardson Bay during the event with some becoming beached off Strawberry Point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of weather systems bringing heavy rain, strong winds, high surf, and a tornado affected the District on March 16-21, 2011. Two deaths occurred during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 10:20 pm Saturday night a huge oak tree, six feet in diameter, fell onto a SUV killing a 46 year-old man.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of weather systems bringing heavy rain, strong winds, high surf, and a tornado affected the District on March 16-21, 2011. Two deaths occurred during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Route 121 was closed from the Junction of State Route 12 to Eighth Street in Schellville due to flooding. There is no information when the roadway reopened.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of weather systems bringing heavy rain, strong winds, high surf, and a tornado affected the District on March 16-21, 2011. Two deaths occurred during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Route 9 was closed to northbound traffic at the Junction of State Route 35 due to a mud slide. There was no information when the roadway was reopened.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of systems affected the San Francisco and Monterey Bay areas with heavy rain and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused numerous slides as well as downed trees and power lines. On March 24 the following areas were affected: Alta Via Road at Moonridge was closed because of a mudslide; Blue Ridge Drive was closed because of downed wires; Buzzard Lagoon Road past address 117 was closed because of a mudslide with downed trees and wires; Eureka Canyon Road was closed because of downed trees and wires; Glenwood Drive at Weston Drive was closed because of a slide; Glenwood Drive at Northridge Drive was closed because of a downed tree and wires; Nelson Road at address 231 was closed because of a mudslide and downed tree; a tree fell on a car on East Zayante Road near Quail Hollow Road; a tree fell on a Jeep near La Madrona Road and Sims Drive; Trees were reported to have fallen into houses at 401 Aptos Creek Road, 523 Santa Margarita Drive and the 400 block of Monterey Drive, all in Aptos and at a house on Moran Way and East Cliff Drive in Live Oak.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of systems affected the San Francisco and Monterey Bay areas with heavy rain and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rock slide occurred above Nelson Road outside of Scotts Valley. Approximately 28 people live above the slide; however, no homes were, or will be threatened if the slide should continue. Rocks covered about 200 yards of roadway isolating the neighborhood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of systems affected the San Francisco and Monterey Bay areas with heavy rain and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Both directions of SR-1 at milepost 20.5, in the Monterey Area, were closed due to a mudslide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of systems affected the San Francisco and Monterey Bay areas with heavy rain and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Route 130 at Mt Hamilton Rd was closed 9 miles west of San Jose at Quimby Rd due to a rock slide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of systems affected the San Francisco and Monterey Bay areas with heavy rain and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain saturated Pleasanton Ridge slid causing a water main break leaving 160 residents without potable water.

CZ_NAME: SOUTHERN MONTEREY BAY AND BIG SUR COAST
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of systems affected the San Francisco and Monterey Bay areas with heavy rain and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and high surf caused widespread flooding, mudslides, road closures, and downed power lines across Monterey County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A system moving through Oregon and far northern California brought gusty winds and light precipitation to the region on the 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph were measured on the 10th. An isolated high wind gust to 61 mph was recorded at the Surprise Valley RAWS at 0830PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the 6th and 7th, a low pressure trough brought some rain and mountain snow to the area, but the main threat was strong and damaging winds in much of Kern County, including the desert and mountain areas. On the 6th, Bakersfield experienced gusty winds up to 35 mph as the cold front passed over the area. Wind gusts reached over 80 mph at some locations in the desert on the 7th. On this day, the winds were strongest during the morning and afternoon hours, when pressure gradients and upper level winds were most favorable. at 1130 AM a roof blew off a house just south of Weldon. A total of 5 semi truck trailers were blown over near Mojave and the intersection of Highways 58 and 14. At Edwards AFB, high winds caused damages to a house, car, and a garage. Also just to the west of Rosamond, several power poles were snapped and toppled due to the high winds. ||Winds subsided during the evening hours, but a few locations in the desert saw gusts continuing at 50-70 mph. By the 8th, temperatures remained several degrees cooler before then next ridge of high pressure strengthened over the area.

The highest amounts, which occurred in Yosemite National Park, ranged from about 1.5 to 3 inches; this was the most precipitation that had occurred in the region for the month thus far. Snow even fell in the foothills, with 5 inches falling at Mid Pines at an elevation of 2400 feet. The main part of the storm arrived on the 20th, bringing record rain to both Bakersfield and Fresno, the latter city recording 1.82 inch of rain. Up to 4 feet of snow fell at Frazier Park; the strong winds created drifts as high as 6 feet deep. Snow caused the closure of Interstate 5 over the Grapevine, and snow even fell on the Temblors and Diablo Range along the west side of the San Joaquin Valley. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A magnitude 9.1 earthquake struck Japan on March 10th, sending a tsunami toward the west coast of the U.S. The tsunami arrived in Southern California in the form of a series of tidal surges, the first observed around 9 am March 11th. The main damage was one bait barge in Mission Bay that broke loosed by the strong currents and superficial damage to a few nearby boats as a result. Surge amplitudes of 2 feet at San Diego and 1.7 feet at La Jolla were measured, as well as a maximum difference in water level near 4 feet at San Diego (surge and retreat). The peak surge at San Diego was at 1 am March 12 when a barge was reported to break loose again in Mission Bay. The beaches did have a tsunami warning posted and a lot of people gathered to observed despite the lifeguards warning. Media coverage was extensive with live helicopter footage during the morning of the 11th, and frequent break-ins for live beach coverage from Orange to San Diego Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mission Bay saw the first tsunami surge around 0915 PST and continued to experience surges every 1.5 to 2 hours. These surges broke apart a bait barge and sent 8 of its 26 bait boxes into the bay where they were lost.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the frequently stormy period that began on the 18th and ended on the 28th, as much as 7 inches of rain fell in the San Joaquin Valley and up to 10 inches of rain drenched the adjacent foothills while the higher elevations of the Sierra (above 7000 feet) received snow accumulations of up to 15 feet. Excessive runoff, combined with an increase in reservoir releases at the major dams caused higher than normal flows along rivers and streams through the end of the month. The Merced River at Stevinson peaked a half of a foot below flood stage at 4:30 pm on the 21st but otherwise remained well above monitor stage during the last two weeks of the month. Water levels along the San Joaquin River approached levels that had not been observed since January, 1997. At Newman, the river first rose above monitor stage on the morning of the 23rd and remained above monitor stage for the remainder of the month. Water flowed into the Fisherman���s Bend trailer park along the San Joaquin River near Newman by the 28th where voluntary evacuations were conducted. In Merced County, several water swollen creeks briefly flowed out of their banks on the morning of the 20th and caused minor flooding.

CZ_NAME: SAN BERNARDINO AND RIVERSIDE COUNTY VALLEYS - THE INLAND EMPIRE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This upper-level low pressure system developed right on the heels of the last and became a closed low over the Northern Baja for 2 days, drawing moisture back into the area along with strong, widespread offshore winds. This system also set off intense showers and isolated thunderstorms with pea-sized hail (accumulations in Rancho Cucamonga and Mission Viejo), as well as several funnel clouds spotted east of John Wayne Airport. Most of the rain with this system was confined to Orange County, the Inland Empire and the northern mountains. Heavy rain was observed in Orange County and the Inland Empire on the 15th and 16th with locations there recording between one-quarter and one-half inch.  The snow level dropped to around 3500 ft., with accumulations of 3 to 8 inches there. Bear Mountain resort recorded 12 to 16 inches. Strong winds were also observed with this storm, especially on the 16th, which was a more widespread wind event than early December, impacting all counties, including San Diego County, with warning-level winds. Several wind gusts of 45-65 mph were reported in the Santa Ana Mountains, the Inland Empire and San Diego County Mountains. Several trees and power poles were downed, leaving many without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds began at the Highland Springs RAWS between 1316 and 1416 PST on the 16th and ended between 1516 and 1616 PST. During this time a peak gust of 63 mph was recorded between 1416 and 1516.|Several trees and power lines were reported down in the Hemet area of Riverside County shortly after this report.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bear Mountain Resort near Big Bear City reported a 24 hour snowfall total of 12 to 16 inches at 7500 feet elevation, approximately half of which fell between 0700 and 1300 PST on the 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This event was an upper-level low that followed an inside slider trajectory, passing just north of the region and providing gusty north-northeast winds on the 22nd and 23rd, but little in the way of precipitation. The hardest hit areas were the Cajon Pass (I-15 through the San Bernardino Mountains) and areas below that in the Inland Empire which recorded wind gusts in the 45 to 65 mph range, with at least one gust in the 70s. Strong winds blew over several semi trucks, one causing injuries to the driver. Several high wind gusts were also reported nearby. As the winds turned more northeast-east on the 23rd into the 24th, San Diego County was affected as well, though most winds did not exceed high wind criteria. Instead the mountains in that area experienced wind gusts in the 40 to 55 mph range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds began between 0551 and 0651 PST on the 22nd at the Fremont Canyon RAWS and ended between 1451 and 1551 PST the same day. During this time, wind gusts of 70 mph or greater were measured each hour for approximately 7 hours, with a peak gust of 75 mph between 0751 and 0851 PST. Sustained winds were 50 mph or greater during many of these hours as well.

CZ_NAME: SAN BERNARDINO AND RIVERSIDE COUNTY VALLEYS - THE INLAND EMPIRE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: These events were the result of a large upper-level closed low over northern Baja, Mexico and featured four Santa Ana events, the strongest of which occurred in Orange County and southwest San Bernardino County with wind gusts 45 to 65+ mph and reported damage on December 1. These winds were strong enough to overturn several trucks, down power lines, reduce visibilities in dust and topple hundreds of large trees in Rancho Cucamonga (near Fontana, San Bernardino and Ontario), near the Cajon Pass along I-15. Rancho Cucamonga was declared a state of emergency by the State of California for the nearly 500 trees damaged. Overall, approximately 400 tons of debris was mitigated in the Rancho Cucamonga area.This area also suffered damage to the electrical infrastructure leaving many without power. As far as snow goes, 15 to 20 inches occurred in the San Bernardino County Mountains near Big Bear and Onyx Peak December 1 to 2, while only a dusting occurred in those mountains west of I-15. Much drier air moved into San Diego County on Saturday December 3, when the Santa Ana mountains and foothills had another surge of Santa Ana/offshore winds with gusts to near 60 mph and very low relative humidity. The final period of winds developed behind another system passing to our east on Monday, bringing wind gusts to 50 to 65 mph Monday night and Tuesday morning in the San Diego County mountains, and very low humidity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Palm Avenue Elementary weather station in the city of San Bernardino recorded sustained northeast winds of 49 mph around 0700 PST on the 1st. A max gust of 70 mph was also recorded at that station at 0551 PST. |The Cal State San Bernardino mesonet station reported high winds between 0350 and 0550 PST. During this time, sustained winds of 40 mph were recorded along with gusts of 47 and 53 mph.|Numerous occurrences of wind damage near these stations were reported. An overturned box truck was blocking the slow lane of I-15 near Glen Helen Parkway.  Power lines and trees were downed near the city of San Bernardino. Power poles and lines were also down across 3rd Avenue near the Crafton Reservoir in Mentone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first notable storm of the winter season swept across Northern California in early October.  The storm was relatively mild for a winter storm but all trans-Sierra passes received some snowfall and gusty winds resulted in poor visibilities along the higher roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow fell sporatically above 7500 feet through much of the day on 10/4/11 before becoming heavier by early on 10/5/11 with snow levels dropping below 6000 feet.  It tapered off late in the day but lingered into 10/6/11 in some areas.  A foot or more of snow fell at the higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada Mountains and was accompanied by gusty winds up to 60 mph.  All trans-Sierra passes received some snowfall and visibilities were poor at times due to the gusty winds.  Eight inches of snow fell at Kingvale and Echo Summit, and four inches fell at Sonora Pass.  Snow fell down to near 5000 feet in some locations.

CZ_NAME: SAN BERNARDINO AND RIVERSIDE COUNTY VALLEYS - THE INLAND EMPIRE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This event was an upper-level low that followed an inside slider trajectory, passing just north of the region and providing gusty north-northeast winds on the 22nd and 23rd, but little in the way of precipitation. The hardest hit areas were the Cajon Pass (I-15 through the San Bernardino Mountains) and areas below that in the Inland Empire which recorded wind gusts in the 45 to 65 mph range, with at least one gust in the 70s. Strong winds blew over several semi trucks, one causing injuries to the driver. Several high wind gusts were also reported nearby. As the winds turned more northeast-east on the 23rd into the 24th, San Diego County was affected as well, though most winds did not exceed high wind criteria. Instead the mountains in that area experienced wind gusts in the 40 to 55 mph range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds began gusting over 40 mph at the Cal State San Bernardino mesonet station around 0350 PST on the 22nd and tapered off around 0850 PST the same morning. During that time a peak gust of 56 mph was recorded between 0700 and 0800 PST. |Several gusts between 45 and 55 mph occurred.  A sustained wind measurement of 43 mph was also reported for the hour between 0650 and 0750 PST. ||Damages included a semi overturned on Interstate 15 near Fontana and another big rig overturned in strong winds on Interstate 210 near Rancho Cucamonga. A third truck was overturned on Interstate 15 near Devore by a strong wind gust. The driver of this box truck suffered minor injuries as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This upper-level low pressure system developed right on the heels of the last and became a closed low over the Northern Baja for 2 days, drawing moisture back into the area along with strong, widespread offshore winds. This system also set off intense showers and isolated thunderstorms with pea-sized hail (accumulations in Rancho Cucamonga and Mission Viejo), as well as several funnel clouds spotted east of John Wayne Airport. Most of the rain with this system was confined to Orange County, the Inland Empire and the northern mountains. Heavy rain was observed in Orange County and the Inland Empire on the 15th and 16th with locations there recording between one-quarter and one-half inch.  The snow level dropped to around 3500 ft., with accumulations of 3 to 8 inches there. Bear Mountain resort recorded 12 to 16 inches. Strong winds were also observed with this storm, especially on the 16th, which was a more widespread wind event than early December, impacting all counties, including San Diego County, with warning-level winds. Several wind gusts of 45-65 mph were reported in the Santa Ana Mountains, the Inland Empire and San Diego County Mountains. Several trees and power poles were downed, leaving many without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple funnel cloud reports from the KSNA ASOS at 10 and 15 miles east of John Wayne Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong surface high pressure built into the Northwest States late on November 30 and into December 1 as an upper low moved south through Nevada and southeastern California. The surface high combined with strong northeast flow aloft over the Sierra to bring very strong winds near the crest with gusty winds below the crest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Elevations below 8500 feet gusted between 40 and 52 mph on the 1st. However, winds above 8500 to 9000 feet near the crest blew much stronger with sustained winds of 50 to 80 mph with gusts of at least 135 to 155 mph (top wind gust of 154 mph recorded). Most of the damage from winds was near the Mammoth Lakes Pack Station (Madera County) where the U.S. Forest Service reported that several hundred trees were blown down. See Storm Data from the National Weather Service in Hanford for more information.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: These events were the result of a large upper-level closed low over northern Baja, Mexico and featured four Santa Ana events, the strongest of which occurred in Orange County and southwest San Bernardino County with wind gusts 45 to 65+ mph and reported damage on December 1. These winds were strong enough to overturn several trucks, down power lines, reduce visibilities in dust and topple hundreds of large trees in Rancho Cucamonga (near Fontana, San Bernardino and Ontario), near the Cajon Pass along I-15. Rancho Cucamonga was declared a state of emergency by the State of California for the nearly 500 trees damaged. Overall, approximately 400 tons of debris was mitigated in the Rancho Cucamonga area.This area also suffered damage to the electrical infrastructure leaving many without power. As far as snow goes, 15 to 20 inches occurred in the San Bernardino County Mountains near Big Bear and Onyx Peak December 1 to 2, while only a dusting occurred in those mountains west of I-15. Much drier air moved into San Diego County on Saturday December 3, when the Santa Ana mountains and foothills had another surge of Santa Ana/offshore winds with gusts to near 60 mph and very low relative humidity. The final period of winds developed behind another system passing to our east on Monday, bringing wind gusts to 50 to 65 mph Monday night and Tuesday morning in the San Diego County mountains, and very low humidity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began falling in the eastern sections of the San Bernardino County Mountains on the evening of November 30th, reached winter storm criteria sometime Thursday evening, December 1st and ceased later that night near midnight. Big Bear City received a storm total of 16 inches. Nearby Big Bear Lake received 18 inches, with some snow drifts reaching 2 to 3 feet. Onyx Peak received a storm total of 16 inches around 8200 feet.|Power lines were also downed in nearby Crestline on Crest Forest Drive at Scenic Drive.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted to 70 mph in the Santa Cruz Mountains downing trees and power lines. This included a critical transmission line near Castle Rock State Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Instability ahead of an approaching upper-level low pressure system set off several waterspouts off the coast of the La Jolla area as well as La Mesa on the 12th. As the system moved onshore, heavy rain was observed in most areas, with many locations west of the mountains and below 4000 ft. receiving two-day totals of 1-1.5 inches. Localized urban/street flooding occurred in San Diego County on SR-94, just west of I-15. The snow level dropped to around 3500 ft., providing the San Diego County Mountains with local snow accumulations of 6-14 inches above 5000 ft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported a funnel cloud southwest of the city of La Mesa around 1625 PST on the 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An inside slider upper-level low pressure system, passing just north of southern California and then across the Great Basin, resulted in strong sustained and gusty northerly winds across much of the Inland Empire and Santa Ana Mountains. Several sites recorded sustained winds of 40-50 mph with gusts over 60 mph. Minor wind damage was reported with this event. A traffic light was blown down in Rancho Cucamonga and a sign blown down onto the freeway near Yorba Linda. A few small brush fires also broke out near Rincon and along the 15 freeway in San Diego County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds began at the Fremont Canyon RAWS between 2230 and 2330 PST on the 1st and ended between 1030 and 1130 PST on the 2nd. During that time, a peak gust of 81 mph was measured between 0330 and 0430 PST on the 2nd.|Nearby, a large sign was blown down on the 91 freeway near Highway 55.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level low pressure system off coast of the Pacific Northwest trailed a cold front through Southern California, bringing 3 days of widespread moderate to heavy rainfall, along with gusty winds. Rainfall amounts over this three-day period were substantial, but did not cause significant flooding. Many areas west of the mountains received 1 to 2 inches of rain, and locally higher where thunderstorms occurred. The snow level, for this first significant snowfall of the season, lowered to between 3500 and 4000 feet, dropping 2 to 6 inches of snow to the mountains above that elevation. As a result, widespread chain control was put in place for these mountain locations. Cold, unstable air behind the front set off scattered thunderstorms as well. The storms near the Banning Pass had accumulations of several inches of pea-sized hail. Despite the high rainfall totals, no significant flooding occurred with this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This storm marked the first significant snow of the season for the San Bernardino County Mountains. A spotter in Running Springs reported 5 inches for the storm total, while another spotter in Forest Falls reported a storm total of 4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, cut-off low pressure system dropped south along the Southern California coast and moved directly across San Diego and northern Baja, bringing 2 days of widespread moderate to heavy rainfall, along with gusty winds, mostly on the 12th. Rainfall amounts over this two-day period were substantial, with locally heavy rainfall rates, providing northern areas west of the mountains with .25 to .50 inch of rain, and one to two inches over the vast majority of San Diego County, even spilling over into the deserts. Lindbergh Field had a peak rainfall rate of 0.40 inches per hour and nearly reached their daily rainfall record with a daily total of 1.4 inches. Urban-type street flooding occurred with this system, dumping up to 1000 cfs of water into the San Diego River on the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CBS 8 via Twitter reported a large tree down on power lines leaving 1500 customers without power. There were no nearby wind reports, but nearby gusts were measured around 40 mph out of the east.

Damages from December 1-3, 2011 will be in the December, 2011 StormData summary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds were blamed for speeding a fire that destroyed two homes and damaged a third in North Livermore near May Nissen Park. Even though the fire's cause is under investigation high winds had a significant effect on this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Great fire started around 1153 PST on the 1st, on the south side of Highway 78 at Scissors Crossing, near Shelter Valley. During the firefight, Highway 78 between San Felipe Rd in Scissors Crossing and Wynola Rd was closed. Only one firefighter sustained minor injuries and no structures or homes were threatened or damaged. The fire burned a total of 2,135 acres before being fully contained around 0600 PST on the 6th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind has been blamed for spreading a fire that damaged 10 condominium units in the Walnut Creek area (200 block of County Brook Loop near Alcosta Blvd). Four other two bedroom condos had less serious damage. One resident was injured when they jumped out of their burning condo to the ground. The cause of the fire is unknown, but wind played a large part in spreading the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper level low pressure system dropped south along the Southern California coast and moved inland across San Diego and northern Baja, bringing 2 days of widespread heavy rainfall, along with gusty winds ahead of the precipitation and mountain snow. Rainfall amounts from the 20th-21st were 1 to 1.5 inches west of the mountains and areas below the snow level of around 5000 feet, resulting in an Urban and Small Stream flood advisory due to minor flooding on freeway ramps and other streets in western San Diego County. The month of November also ended around 300% of normal for monthly rainfall at Lindbergh Field, due in part to this event. Above 5000 feet, many areas received significant snow amounts, the highest being above 7000 feet in the San Bernardino County Mountains. Above 7000 feet, the Mountain High ski resort received 10 inches of snow with this storm, while the Onyx Peak and Wrightwood areas received 4-6 inches of snow. Big Bear and other areas between 6000 and 7000 feet received 2-4 inches. All of the areas above the snow level had chain controls in place for this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Weather Currents station at Beaumont-Calimesa received 1.17 inches of rain in the hour between 1900 and 2000 PST on the 20th and a total of 1.61 inches for the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of slow moving thunderstorms produced locally heavy rain across eastern Riverside county shortly after midnight on the 6th of July. Flooding of roads and washes resulted from the heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cluster of slow moving thunderstorms moved westward across far eastern Riverside county shortly after midnight on the 6th of July. The storms affected the area around Blythe. A trained weather spotter reported very heavy rainfall with rain rates estimated to be approximately 2 inches per hour. The spotter also indicated that area washes were flooding, as well as streets in the community of Blythe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong thunderstorm developed south of Imperial near the Mexican border during the afternoon on the 7th of July. Strong microburst winds and heavy rain impacted the community of Calexico, damaging homes, trees and power poles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm with damaging microburst winds moved through the community of Calexico during the late afternoon on July 7th. As reported by law enforcement personnel, wind gusts estimated to be at least 60 knots toppled at least 6 power poles. The winds also knocked over several trees in central Calexico. In addition, heavy rain associated with the thunderstorm caused street flooding in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving thunderstorm producing very heavy rain moved across Joshua Tree National Park during the afternoon on the 7th of July. The heavy rain led to flash flooding in the park, closing the north entrance.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms developed across Joshua Tree National Park during the early afternoon hours on July 7th. The storms produced locally heavy rainfall, estimated by radar to be in excess of 1.5 inches. At 330 pm, Park and Forest service personnel reported flash flooding at Pinto Wye and near 49 Palm Oasis in the park. Flooding prompted the closure of the north entrance to the park.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public in Tionesta reported 10.0 inches of snow, presumably in 24 hours or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper level low pressure system dropped south along the Southern California coast and moved inland across San Diego and northern Baja, bringing 2 days of widespread heavy rainfall, along with gusty winds ahead of the precipitation and mountain snow. Rainfall amounts from the 20th-21st were 1 to 1.5 inches west of the mountains and areas below the snow level of around 5000 feet, resulting in an Urban and Small Stream flood advisory due to minor flooding on freeway ramps and other streets in western San Diego County. The month of November also ended around 300% of normal for monthly rainfall at Lindbergh Field, due in part to this event. Above 5000 feet, many areas received significant snow amounts, the highest being above 7000 feet in the San Bernardino County Mountains. Above 7000 feet, the Mountain High ski resort received 10 inches of snow with this storm, while the Onyx Peak and Wrightwood areas received 4-6 inches of snow. Big Bear and other areas between 6000 and 7000 feet received 2-4 inches. All of the areas above the snow level had chain controls in place for this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds began in the Coachella Valley between 0950 and 1050 PST and ended between 1250 and 1350 PST on the 19th. During that time, two gusts of 58 mph were measured at the Whitewater RAWS between 0950 and 1150 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system spread considerable moisture into southeast California, and led the development of scattered thunderstorms over Riverside county on the 13th of September. The moisture content in the atmosphere was very high and the thunderstorms produced flash flooding during the morning hours. Some of the flooding occurred in Joshua Tree National Park, fortunately there were no injuries or fatalities caused by the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential rain developed across Joshua Tree National Park during the morning hours on September 13. Park employee estimates along with radar rainfall estimates indicated that at least 3 inches of rain fell in the park with the thunderstorms. Joe Zarki, Public Information Officer for the park, stated that extensive flood damage occurred to Park Route 11, the Pinto Basin Road, with loss of pavement in numerous areas. The Cottonwood Visitor's Center was flooded and parts of the Cottonwood Campground were washed away. No visitors or park employees were injured during the flash flooding and no vehicles were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds affected the San Francisco Bay and Monterey Bay area causing damage and one fatality. The fatality occurred on November 30, 2011 and is in the November, 2011 StormData summary. Damages from November 30, 2011 are also in the November, 2011 StormData summary. Damages from December 1-3, 2011 will be in the December, 2011 StormData summary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind-driven fire destroyed a large barn in Santa Rosa and spread to two sheds and destroyed several vehicles. The fire also destroyed motorcycles, pickups, a riding lawn mower, and an RV. The fire was sparked by power lines that were blown to the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long lasting winter storm moved through northern California during this interval.  The snow level started off above pass level with heavy rain totals reported at lower elevations.  The snow level lowered to populated areas later in the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter ESE Mount Shasta City reported 7.0 inches of snow in 10 hours ending at 17/1300 PST.  A spotter SSE Mount Shasta City reported 8 inches of snow in about 18 hours ending at 17/1130 PST. The cooperative observer in Mount Shasta City reported 9.0 inches of snow in 5.5 hours ending at 17/1130 PST, then 14.0 inches in 24 hours ending at 17/2000 PST. The cooperative observer at Dunsmuir reported 24 hour snow totals of 5.0 inches at 17/0800 PST and 5 .0 more inches at 18/0800 PST.  A spotter 6NW Mount Shasta City reported 12.0 inches of snow in 12 hours ending at 17/1430 PST.  A spotter 4SW Weed reported 11.5 inches of snow in 16 hours ending at 18/0000 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved quickly across Northern California, bringing snow down to 4000 feet and a foot or more of snow to the Sierra Nevada passes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of heavy snow fell above 4000 feet and resulted in up to a foot of snow falling near 6000 feet and more than a foot of snow at the highest elevations.  Eleven inches of snow fell at Kingvale on Interstate 80 and eight inches of snow fell over Echo Summit.  Locally gusty winds resulted in poor visibilities in some areas but mostly at the pass level.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed across portions of Imperial county during the afternoon hours on October 3rd. The storms generated strong and gusty outflow winds in excess of 50 mph which were sufficient to blow down a number of trees and power poles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms near El Centro generated strong and gusty outflow winds during the afternoon hours on October 3rd. The California highway patrol reported that power poles and live power lines were blown down along Bowker road near Interstate 8. The poles were blown down by the outflow winds which were estimated to be at least 60 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed across portions of Imperial county during the afternoon hours on October 3rd. The storms generated strong and gusty outflow winds in excess of 50 mph which were sufficient to blow down a number of trees and power poles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed across Imperial county during the afternoon hours on October 3rd. The storms generated gusty outflow winds estimated to be at least 50 knots. According to the California Highway Patrol, the strong winds blew down a number of large trees across the road near the intersection of Ross and Bowker roads, about 5 miles east of El Centro. The Highway Patrol did not mention the diameter of the trees that were downed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with locally heavy rainfall developed across portions of central Riverside county, from Joshua Tree National Park south to the Salton Sea. Radar estimated that at least 3 inches of rain fell in areas west of Chiriaco Summit since 6 am. The heavy rain led to flash flooding near the north side of the Salton Sea, east of Desert Beach. Law enforcement personnel reported that the flash flooding washed out a bridge on Highway 111 and Desert Beach Drive.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall across portions of central Riverside county, including the area around Desert Center, during the morning hours on September 13. Thunderstorms started in the mid morning, and trained over the same area for several hours. Radar estimated that 3 to 4 inches of rain fell with the storms, and this led to flash flooding. At around 1220 pm, Department of Highways personnel reported that water was flowing across State Route 177 between mile markers 7 and 10.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This upper-level low pressure system developed right on the heels of the last and became a closed low over the Northern Baja for 2 days, drawing moisture back into the area along with strong, widespread offshore winds. This system also set off intense showers and isolated thunderstorms with pea-sized hail (accumulations in Rancho Cucamonga and Mission Viejo), as well as several funnel clouds spotted east of John Wayne Airport. Most of the rain with this system was confined to Orange County, the Inland Empire and the northern mountains. Heavy rain was observed in Orange County and the Inland Empire on the 15th and 16th with locations there recording between one-quarter and one-half inch.  The snow level dropped to around 3500 ft., with accumulations of 3 to 8 inches there. Bear Mountain resort recorded 12 to 16 inches. Strong winds were also observed with this storm, especially on the 16th, which was a more widespread wind event than early December, impacting all counties, including San Diego County, with warning-level winds. Several wind gusts of 45-65 mph were reported in the Santa Ana Mountains, the Inland Empire and San Diego County Mountains. Several trees and power poles were downed, leaving many without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds began at the Fremont Canyon RAWS between 0351 and 0451 PST on the 16th and ended between 1351 and 1451 PST the same day. During that time a max gust of 65 mph was measured, along with several other gusts above 58 mph (including 2 of 61 mph and one of 60 mph).||During this time, power poles were reported down by Fox 11 News in nearby Yorba Linda. Around 240 customers were reported without power in nearby Tustin as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wind event from November 30, 2011 to December 2, 2011 caused damage and one fatality across the San Francisco and Monterey Bay areas. The fatality occurred on November 30, 2011. Wind damage occurred from November 30, 2011 through December 2, 2011.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds snapped a power pole on Old Redwood Highway in the Larkfield area north of Santa Rosa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds affected the San Francisco Bay and Monterey Bay area causing damage and one fatality. The fatality occurred on November 30, 2011 and is in the November, 2011 StormData summary. Damages from November 30, 2011 are also in the November, 2011 StormData summary. Damages from December 1-3, 2011 will be in the December, 2011 StormData summary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds gusting to up to 70 mph felled trees and power lines in both Scotts Valley and the San Lorenzo Valley. Electricity was cut to Scotts Valley and resulted in several small fires. 25,000 homes and businesses were without power. Damage included a large tree that crushed the roof of a home on the 100 block of Baja Sol Drive in Scotts Valley and another tree that fell on a home on the 400 block of Lockwood Lane. Trees also took down a transmission line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shallow marine layer resulted in areas of dense fog along the coastal areas in Orange County, the San Diego coastal mesas and the Western San Diego County valleys. Visibility was a below a quarter mile in many locations for several hours in the overnight and early morning periods from the 29th through the 31st. Operations at John Wayne and San Diego International Airports were impacted for several hours both nights.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The John Wayne Airport ASOS reported visibility of less than one-quarter mile from 2318 PST on the 29th through 0439 PST on the morning of the 30th. Visibility of one-eighth of a mile was reported from 2229 to 2317 PST on the night of the 30th and again on the morning of the 31st from 0553 to 0652 PST and from 0721 to 0947 PST. Visibility of one-eighth of a mile was again reported the evening of the 31st from 1823 to 1852 PST and again from 2053 to 2352 PST.|The Newport Beach lifeguards also reported visibility of less than 100 yards on the morning of the 31st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front brought high winds to many parts of northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Indian Well RAWS recorded a gust to 61 mph at 13/1316 PDT and a gust to 66 mph at 13/1616 PDT.  The SHF07 portable RAWS recorded a gust to 65 mph at 13/1529 PDT and a gust to 69 mph at 13/1829 PDT.  The Van Bremmer RAWS recorded a gust to 58 mph at 13/1145 PDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front brought high winds to many parts of northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Timber Mountain RAWS recorded a gust to 59 mph at 13/1412 PDT and a gust to 58 mph at 13/1812 PDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Onion Wildfire was started by lightning on 08/19/2011.  By the time it was contained around 09/10/2011, it had burned 560 acres.  No fire fighting cost estimates were available.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wind event from November 30, 2011 to December 2, 2011 caused damage and one fatality across the San Francisco and Monterey Bay areas. The fatality occurred on November 30, 2011. Wind damage occurred from November 30, 2011 through December 2, 2011.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds toppled a tree across the northbound lanes of US 101 near SR 237 in Sunnyvale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm over the Warner Mountains west of Cedarville produced a very strong but localized downburst in Cedarville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Modoc County Record reported that strong winds came down from Cedar Pass and snapped a 30-inch and a 16-inch diameter tree near Cedarville. A building on one ranch had a 20 foot by 34 foot piece of roof blown off. Large farm equipment (swather) was pushed and numerous 110 pound bales of hay were rolled or knocked over in the winds.|The highest measured gust was 55 mph at the Surprise Valley RAWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: July began with an upper-level ridge building into California in the wake of the storms that marked the last days of June. Temperatures warmed to near normal on the first day of the month, and continued to warm the next several days. Bakersfield recorded 9 consecutive days of triple digit heat from July 2nd-10th, and Fresno had 8 consecutive days. Both cities had highs of 106 degrees on July 4th (the hottest day of the period), with Bakersfield repeating the next day.
